sandbox

  1. Locally trivial vector bundles
  2. Locally free sheaves

Prerequisites

Locally trivial vector bundles

If $M$ is a manifold, we saw earlier that the tangent bundle $T(M)$ is locally trivial, in the sense that $M$ is covered by open sets $V$ such that there is a local trivialization

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha: V \times \bbr^n \to \pi^{-1}(V). \end{equation}

Here, $\pi$ is the natural smooth projection map $\pi:T(M) \to M$, and to say that $\alpha$ is a local trivialization means that it is a diffeomorphism of smooth manifolds which restricts to a linear isomorphism on each fiber. These considerations inspire and motivate the following abstract structure:

Definition. A locally trivial vector bundle is a surjective smooth map $\pi:E \to M$ of smooth manifolds with the following properties:

  1. The map $\pi$ is a set-theoretic vector bundle, so that each fiber \begin{equation}\notag E_p \defeq \pi^{-1}(p) \end{equation} is a finite-dimensional $\bbr$-vector space.
  2. The manifold $M$ is covered in open sets $V$ for which there exists a diffeomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \alpha: V \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(V) \end{equation} which is also a homomorphism of set-theoretic vector bundles over $V$. Here, $V\times \bbr^k$ is viewed as the total set of the trivial set-theoretic vector bundle with base $V$. Such a diffeomorphism $\alpha$ is called a local trivialization (over $V$).

If each vector space $E_p$ has a fixed (finite) dimension $k$, then $\pi$ is called a rank-$k$ locally trivial vector bundle.

If $\pi’:E’ \to M$ is a second locally trivial vector bundle, then a homomorphism of vector bundles is a function $\alpha:E \to E’$ which is a homomorphism of set-theoretic vector bundles and which is smooth. Remember that the first condition means that $\alpha$ restricts to a linear transformation

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha_p \defeq \alpha|_{E_p}: E_p \to E’_p \end{equation}

on each fiber. In particular, we require that $\alpha(E_p) \subseteq E_p’$, for all $p\in M$.

We shall say that $\alpha:E\to E’$ is an isomorphism of locally trivial vector bundles if there exists a homomorphism $\beta:E’\to E$ of locally trivial vector bundles such that $\beta \circ \alpha = \id_E$ and $\alpha \circ \beta = \id_{E’}$.

In the locally trivial case, the manifolds $E$ and $M$, which were called the total set and base set in the context of set-theoretic vector bundles, are now called the total space and base space.

Since locally trivial vector bundles will be the primary types of vector bundles we will consider henceforth, we will often drop the modifier “locally trivial” and simply refer to vector bundles. Also, though technically we have defined a vector bundle to be a certain type of surjective map $\pi:E \to M$, we will often refer to vector bundle by referencing only the total space $E$.

One of the most useful properties of homomorphisms of vector bundles is that the isomorphism property may be tested locally on the fibers. Precisely:

Fiberwise Criterion for Vector Bundle Isomorphisms. Let $\alpha: E \to E’$ be a homomorphism of vector bundles over a common base space $M$. Then $\alpha$ is an isomorphism of vector bundles if and only if each induced map $\alpha_p:E_p \to E’_p$ on the fibers is a linear isomorphism.

I suggest that you attempt to prove this theorem on your own. You will see that the local triviality of the vector bundles is used in a crucial way.

Sections and sheaves

In this section we study smooth sections of (locally trivial) vector bundles. We have already seen examples of these gadgets in two places: (1) One guise of smooth vector fields on a manifold is as smooth (global) sections of the tangent bundle, and (2) smooth sections of the trivial vector bundle $M \times \bbr \to M$ are exactly smooth functions on $M$. Indeed, we covered (1) in this post, while to verify that claim (2) is true, we note that a smooth map $\sigma: M \to M \times \bbr$ of the form

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma(p) = (p,f(p)) \end{equation}

is uniquely determined by the smooth function $f\in C^\infty(M)$. In general, the definition is as follows:

Definition. Let $\pi:E \to M$ be a vector bundle and $U\subseteq M$ a nonempty open set. A local section of the vector bundle over $U$ is a function $\sigma:U\to E$ such that $\pi\circ \sigma = \id_U$. We shall say $\sigma$ is a smooth local section if it is a smooth map between manifolds.

We write $\Gamma(U,E)$ for the set of smooth local sections over $U$. If the vector bundle $E$ is clear from context, we will write $\Gamma(U)$ in place of $\Gamma(U,E)$. Sections in $\Gamma(M)$ are called smooth global sections.

We will often write the functional value $\sigma(p) \in E_p$ of a section as $\sigma_p$. If the section happens to have an index, like $\sigma_i$, then we will write $\sigma_i|_p$ in place of $(\sigma_i)_p$.

Exercise. Given two smooth local sections $\sigma,\tau\in \Gamma(U)$, we define their sum, denoted $\sigma+\tau$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (\sigma+\tau)_p = \sigma_p + \tau_p, \end{equation}

where the sum on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. If $f\in C^\infty(U)$ is a function, we define the product of $f$ and $\sigma$, denoted $f\sigma$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (f\sigma)_p = f(p) \sigma_p, \end{equation}

where the product on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. Prove that with respect to these operations, the set $\Gamma(U)$ is a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

A smoothly varying collection of bases of the fibers of a vector bundle is called a smooth local frame, which is terminology that you should recognize from the earlier post on vector fields. Officially:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Then a collection of smooth local sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ is called a smooth local frame over $U$ if

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1|_p,\ldots,\vartheta_k|_p \in E_p \end{equation}

is a basis for all $p\in U$.

One of the main uses of smooth local frames is to test for smoothness of general sections:

Theorem (Componentwise criterion for smoothness). Let $\pi:E\to M$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Given a function $\sigma:U\to E$ (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous) such that $\pi \circ \sigma = \id_U$ and a smooth local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k\in \Gamma(U)$, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p = \sigma^i(p) \vartheta_i|_p \end{equation}

for each $p\in U$, where $\sigma^i: U \to \bbr$ are uniquely determined real-valued functions (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous). These functions are called the component functions of $\sigma$ with respect to the local frame.

Then $\sigma$ is smooth if and only if each component function $\sigma^i$ is smooth.

Exercise. Prove that the local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ forms a basis as a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

Given a manifold $M$, we recall that we proved the assignments $U \mapsto C^\infty(U)$ and $U \mapsto \calx(U)$ are examples of sheaves in this post. Had we known at the time that $\calx(U)$ could be viewed as smooth local sections of a certain vector bundle, then we could have applied the following general result:

Theorem (Sections of vector bundles form sheaves). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$. If for each open set $U\subseteq M$ we define $\cale(U) = \Gamma(U,E)$, then $\cale$ is a $C^\infty_M$-module, where $C^\infty_M$ is the sheaf of smooth functions on $M$. The sheaf $\cale$ is called the sheaf of sections of the vector bundle $E$.

We learned the general definition of a sheaf in the previous post. When we say that $\cale$ is a sheaf of $C^\infty_M$-modules, we really mean that $\cale$ is a sheaf of modules over the sheaf of rings $C^\infty_M$. For the general definition of these latter types of objects, see here.

You also learned the definition of stalks of structure sheaves of ringed spaces in the previous post. It turns out that such stalks may be defined for any sheaf and, in particular, they may be defined for sheaves of sections of vector bundles. You should have no trouble, therefore, in adapting the definition of stalks in the previous post to apply to our sheaves of sections. Otherwise, see here for the official definition.

Let’s set some notation for stalks:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. We will write $\cale_p$ for the stalk of $\cale$ at $p\in M$. Elements in $\cale_p$ will be called germs of sections.

Exercise.

  1. Prove that each stalk $\cale_p$ is a module over the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$ with respect the evident operations. (By now, I’m hoping that this proof is routine!)
  2. For each $p\in M$, recall that there is a well-defined homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \dev_p:C^\infty_{M,p} \to \bbr, \quad f \mapsto f(p), \end{equation} of $\bbr$-algebras. By restricting scalars along this map, a fiber $E_p$ of the vector bundle may be given a $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module structure. With respect to this structure, prove that the map \begin{equation}\notag e_p: \cale_p \to E_p, \quad \sigma \mapsto \sigma_p, \end{equation} is a well-defined homomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules.

Remember that the germs in the stalk $\cale_p$ are actually equivalence classes of sections $\sigma$ defined on open neighborhoods of $p$, so my definition “$\sigma \mapsto \sigma_p$” of the evaluation map $e_p$ is technically an abuse of notation. However, as I’ve mentioned in the past, this is a typical abuse, and going forth I will use it without further comment.

Take care to notice the difference between $\cale_p$ and $E_p$ for a vector bundle $E$ over a manifold $M$. The former is the stalk of the sheaf of sections at the point $p\in M$, while the latter is the fiber over $p$. While these are different objects, there is, nevertheless, a tight link between them:

Theorem. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. For each $p\in M$, let $\mfm_p$ denote the maximal ideal of the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$. The sequence

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \to \mfm_p \to \cale_p \xrightarrow{e_p} E_p \to 0 \end{equation}

is exact, where the first map is the inclusion and the second is the evaluation map.

A proof is in contained in Jet’s book, Chapter 12.

Theorem (Extension theorem for local sections). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$ and each open neighbohrood $U$ of $p$, the natural localization map

\begin{equation}\notag \lambda_{U,p}: \cale(U) \to \cale_p \end{equation}

sending a section to the germ that it represents is surjective.

The proof uses bump functions, just like the same extension theorem for smooth functions.

Theorem (Stalks of vector bundles are free). Let $E$ be a rank-$k$ vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$, the stalk $\cale_p$ is a free $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module of rank $k$. In particular, if $U$ is an open neighborhood of $p$ over which $E$ trivializes, then the natural map

\begin{equation}\label{nat-eqn} C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty(U)} \cale(U) \to \cale_p, \quad f \otimes \sigma \mapsto f\lambda_{U,p}(\sigma), \end{equation}

is an isomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules, where $\lambda_{U,p}$ is the natural localization map from the previous theorem.

We have already observed that a choice of local frame

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \cale(U) \end{equation}

forms a basis of $\cale(U)$ as a $C^\infty(U)$-module, and that the localization map $\lambda_{U,p}$ is surjective. Thus, we need only show that the map \eqref{nat-eqn} is injective. So, suppose that we have an element $f^i \otimes \vartheta_i$ in the domain of \eqref{nat-eqn}, with summation over repeated indices implied. Suppose that it is sent to zero in the stalk, so that we have $f^i \vartheta_i = 0$. (Be very careful to understand exactly what this equation means, as I am confusing sections with germs again, and have omitted the localization map.) By shrinking $U$ to a smaller neighborhood $V$ of $p$ if needed, we may suppose that this equation actually occurs in the module $\cale(V)$, where now $f^i \in C^\infty(V)$. But the sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k$ are linearly independent in $\cale(V)$ as well, and hence $f^i = 0$ on $V$ for each $i$. Hence $f^i=0$ in $C^\infty_{M,p}$, which is what we wanted to prove. Q.E.D.

Construction theorems

It will be very important for us to be able to construct vector bundles and homomorphisms between them efficiently. In order to do this, we will first begin by identifying the sorts of necessary structures that come along with vector bundles and homomorphisms, and then show that these structures are actually sufficient to construct such objects.

Definition. Let $\alpha:M\to N$ be a smooth map, and let

\begin{equation}\notag \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\}_{i,j\in I} \quad \text{and} \quad \{V_i,\sigma_{ij}\}_{i,j\in I} \end{equation}

be a $\GL_n$-cocyle on $M$ and a $\GL_m$-cocycle on $N$, respectively, indexed by the same set $I$, with the property that $\alpha(U_i ) \subseteq V_i$ for each $i$. Then a homomorphism of cocycles over $\alpha$ is a collection $\{A_i\}_{i\in I}$ of smooth maps $A_i: U_i \to \M_{m\times n}$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag A_i(p)\tau_{ij}(p) = \sigma_{ij}(\alpha(p))A_j(p) \end{equation}

for all $p\in U_i \cap U_j$.

Blah!

Vector Bundle Homomorphism Construction Theorem. Let $E$ and $F$ be a rank-$n$ and rank-$m$ vector bundle over manifolds $M$ and $N$, respectively, and suppose $\alpha:M \to N$ is a smooth map. Choose a representative $\GL_n$- and $\GL_m$-cocycle

\begin{equation}\notag \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\}_{i,j\in I} \quad \text{and} \quad \{V_i,\sigma_{ij}\}_{i,j\in I} \end{equation}

of $E$ and $F$, respectively, such that $\alpha(U_i) \subseteq V_i$ for each $i\in I$.

  1. A homomorphism $\theta: E \to F$ of vector bundles over $\alpha$ determines a homomorphism \begin{equation}\label{start-eqn} \{A_i\}_{i\in I} : \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\} \to \{V_i,\sigma_{ij}\} \end{equation} of cocycles over $\alpha$ such that $\theta$ is locally given by the map \begin{equation}\label{local-eqn} U_i \times \bbr^n \to V_i \times \bbr^m, \quad (p,v) \mapsto (\alpha(p), A_i(p)v). \end{equation}
  2. Conversely, suppose that we are given a homomorphism \eqref{start-eqn} of cocycles over $\alpha$. Then there is a homomorphism $\theta:E \to F$ of vector bundles over $\alpha$ which is locally given by the formula \eqref{local-eqn}.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

In the notation of the theorem, given a homomorphism $\theta:E\to F$ of vector bundles over $\alpha$, any homomorphism of cocycles as in (1.) is said to represent the homomorphism $\theta$. Note that this homomorphism of cocycles is not uniquely determined by $\theta$ alone, but rather by $\theta$ and the representative cocycles of the vector bundles.

Pullbacks

Definition. Let $\pi:E \to N$ be a vector bundle in $\SetVect_N$ and $\alpha:M \to N$ a morphism in $\Set$. We define the set

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast E = \bigsqcup_{p\in M} E_{\alpha(p)} \end{equation}

and let $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$ be the canonical projection. Then $\rho$ is a vector bundle in $\SetVect_M$ called the pullback of $E$ along $\alpha$, with fibers

\begin{equation}\notag (\alpha^\ast E)_p = \{p\} \times E_{\alpha(p)}. \end{equation}

Blah!

Theorem (Cocycle representations of smooth pullbacks). Let $\pi:E \to N$ be a rank-$k$ vector bundle in $\Vect_N$ and $\alpha:M \to N$ a morphism in $\Mfld$. Then:

  1. A representative $\GL_k$-cocycle \begin{equation}\label{equiv-eqn} \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\}_{i\in I} \end{equation} of $E$ with trivializations \begin{equation}\notag \Phi_i: U_i \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(U_i) \end{equation} induces a smooth structure on the pullback bundle $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$ in $\SetVect_M$.
  2. This smooth structure has a representative $\GL_k$-cocycle $\{V_i,\sigma_{ij}\}_{i\in I}$ with \begin{equation}\notag V_i = \alpha^{-1}(U_i) \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{ij}: V_i \cap V_j \xrightarrow{\alpha} U_i \cap U_j \xrightarrow{\tau_{ij}} \GL_k \end{equation} and trivializations \begin{equation}\notag \Psi_i: V_i \times \bbr^k \to \rho^{-1}(V_i), \quad (p,v) \mapsto (p, \Phi_i(\alpha(p),v)). \end{equation}
  3. Finally, this smooth structure depends only on the equivalence class of the cocycle \eqref{equiv-eqn}, and hence it only depends on the isomorphism class of $\pi:E \to N$.

Blah!

Theorem (Universal property of set-theoretic pullback bundles). Let $\pi:E \to N$ be a vector bundle in $\SetVect_N$, let $\alpha:M \to N$ be a morphism in $\Set$, and let $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$ be the pullback bundle in $\SetVect_M$.

  1. The function \begin{equation}\notag \tl{\alpha}: \alpha^\ast E \to E, \quad (p,v) \mapsto v, \end{equation} is a well-defined morphism in $\SetVect_\alpha$.
  2. If $\theta: F\to E$ is a morphism in $\SetVect_\alpha$, then the function \begin{equation}\notag \kappa:F\to \alpha^\ast E, \quad x\mapsto (\pi_F(x),\theta(x)), \end{equation} is a well-defined morphism in $\SetVect_M$, where $\pi_F: F\to M$ is the structural projection.
  3. The morphism $\kappa$ is the unique morphism in $\SetVect_M$ for which $\theta = \tl{\alpha}\circ \kappa$.

The proof is trivial. I will leave it to you.

Theorem (Universal property of smooth pullback bundles). Let $\pi:E \to N$ be a vector bundle in $\Vect_N$, let $\alpha:M \to N$ be a morphism in $\Mfld$, and let $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$ be the pullback bundle in $\SetVect_M$. Let $\rho$ have the smooth structure induced by a $\GL_k$-cocycle of $E$. In the notation of that theorem:

  1. The function \begin{equation}\notag \tl{\alpha}: \alpha^\ast E \to E, \quad (p,v) \mapsto v, \end{equation} is a well-defined morphism in $\Vect_\alpha$. It is represented by the morphism of cocycles \begin{equation}\notag \{I_i\}_{i\in I} : \{V_i,\sigma_{ij}\}_{i\in I} \to \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\}_{i\in I} \end{equation} over $\alpha$ with \begin{equation}\notag I_i: V_i \to \M_{k\times k}, \quad I_i(p) = \id_{\bbr^k}, \end{equation} for each $i$.
  2. If $\theta: F\to E$ is a morphism in $\Vect_\alpha$, then the function \begin{equation}\notag \kappa:F\to \alpha^\ast E, \quad x\mapsto (\pi_F(x),\theta(x)), \end{equation} is a well-defined morphism in $\Vect_M$, where $\pi_F: F\to M$ is the structural projection. If $\theta$ is represented by the morphism of cocycles \begin{equation}\notag \{A_i\}_{i\in I} : \{W_i,\chi_{ij}\}_{i\in I} \to \{U_i,\tau_{ij}\}_{i\in I} \end{equation} over $\id_M$, then

for which the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}\alpha^\ast E \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\tl{\alpha}} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\rho} & \color{white} E \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\pi} \\ \color{white} M \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\alpha} & \color{white} N} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes. Furthermore, the set-theoretic vector bundle $\rho$ and map $\tl{\alpha}$ are the universal objects for which this diagram commutes, in the sense that if

\begin{equation}\notag \varsigma: F \to M \end{equation}

is a second set-theoretic vector bundle and $\beta:F \to E$ is a set-theoretic map for which

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}F \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\beta} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\varsigma} & \color{white} E \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\pi} \\ \color{white} M \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\alpha} & \color{white} N} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes, then there is a unique homomorphism $\phi: F\to \alpha^\ast E$ of set-theoretic vector bundles over $M$ for which the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}F \ar@[white]@/^1.0pc/[drr]^{\color{white}\beta} \ar@[white]@/_1.0pc/[ddr]_{\color{white}\varsigma} \ar@[white][dr]^{\color{white}\phi} \\ & \color{white}\alpha^\ast E \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\tl{\alpha}} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\rho} & \color{white} E \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\pi} \\ & \color{white} M \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\alpha} & \color{white} N} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes. We shall use the Vector Bundle Chart Lemma to construct the vector bundle $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$. As a set-theoretic vector bundle, we define

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast E = \bigsqcup_{p\in M} E_{\alpha(p)} \end{equation}

and let $\rho:\alpha^\ast E\to M$ be the natural projection. Notice that $\alpha^\ast E$ is nothing but the fiber product of sets

\begin{equation}\notag M \times_N E \defeq \left\{ (p,v) \in M \times E : \alpha(p) = \pi(v)\right\}. \end{equation}

Suppose that $E$ trivializes over the open cover $\{U_i\}$ of $N$, with trivialization maps

\begin{equation}\notag \Phi_i: U_i \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(U_i). \end{equation}

For each index $i$, set $V_i = \alpha^{-1}(U_i)$, and define the isomorphisms

\begin{equation}\label{yuppers-eqn} \Psi_i: V_i \times \bbr^k \to \rho^{-1}(V_i) = \bigsqcup_{p\in V_i} E_{\alpha(p)}, \quad \Psi_i(p,v) = (p, \Phi_i(\alpha(p),v)), \end{equation}

of set-theoretic vector bundles. If

\begin{equation}\notag \tau_{ij}: U_i \cap U_j \to \GL_k(\bbr) \end{equation}

are the transition maps of $E$, and if $V_i \cap V_j \neq \emptyset$, then the composite

\begin{equation}\notag \Psi^{-1}_i \circ \Psi_j : (V_i\cap V_j) \times \bbr^k \to (V_i\cap V_j) \times \bbr^k \end{equation}

has the form

\begin{equation}\notag (p,v) \mapsto (p, \tau_{ij}(\alpha(p))v). \end{equation}

But the composites

\begin{equation}\label{yuppers2-eqn} \sigma_{ij}: V_i \cap V_j \xrightarrow{\alpha} U_i \cap U_j \xrightarrow{\tau_{ij}} \GL_k(\bbr) \end{equation}

are smooth. Thus, by the Vector Bundle Chart Lemma, the natural surjection $\rho:\alpha^\ast E \to M$ is a rank-$k$ vector bundle with trivializations \eqref{yuppers-eqn} and transition maps \eqref{yuppers2-eqn}.

We define the map

\begin{equation}\notag \tl{\alpha}:\alpha^\ast E \to E, \quad (p,v) \mapsto v. \end{equation}

It fits into commutative diagrams

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white} V_i \times \bbr^k \ar@[white][r]^{\color{white} \alpha \times \id} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\Psi_i} & \color{white} U_i \times \bbr^k \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\Phi_i} \\ \color{white} \rho^{-1}(V_i) \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\tl{\alpha}} & \color{white} \pi^{-1}(U_i) } \end{xy} \end{equation}

along with the trivialization maps, and is thus smooth.

Clearly $\pi\circ \tl{\alpha} = \alpha \circ \rho$, so all we need to do is establish the universal property of the bundle $\rho:\alpha^\ast E\to M$. So, let $\varsigma: F\to M$ be a vector bundle with the property stated in the theorem. We then define the map

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: F \to \alpha^\ast E, \quad \phi(x) = (\varsigma(x), \beta(x)). \end{equation}

This is obviously the unique set-theoretic map for which

\begin{equation}\notag \varsigma = \rho \circ \phi \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \tl{\alpha} \circ \phi, \end{equation}

so we need only prove that $\phi$ is a homomorphism of vector bundles. But if $x\in F_p$ for some $p\in M$, then

\begin{equation}\notag \phi(x) = (p, \beta(x)) \in (\alpha^\ast E)_p = \{p\} \times E_{\alpha(p)}. \end{equation}

Theorem (Identification of stalks of pullbacks). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $N$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$, let $\alpha:M \to N$ be a smooth map, and let $\alpha^\ast \cale$ be the sheaf of sections of the pullback bundle $\alpha^\ast E$. Then for each $p\in M$ with $q=\alpha(p)$, the natural $C^\infty_{M,p}$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \Theta_p: C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \cale_q \to (\alpha^\ast\cale)_p, \quad f \otimes \sigma \mapsto f\alpha^\ast\sigma, \end{equation}

is an isomorphism and fits into the commutative diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \cale_q \ar@[white][r]^-{\color{white}\Theta_p} \ar@[white][d]_{\color{white}\dev_p \otimes e_q} & \color{white} (\alpha^\ast \cale)_p \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}e_p} \\ \color{white} \bbr \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} E_q \ar@[white][r] & \color{white} (\alpha^\ast\cale)_p = E_q } \end{xy} \end{equation}

where $\dev_p,e_p,e_q$ are the evaluation maps and the bottom horizontal map is the natural map.

Direct sums, duals, and tensor products

Derivatives in vector bundles: connections

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$. A Koszul connection on $E$ (or simply a connection) is an $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \Gamma(E) \to \Gamma(E), \quad (X,\sigma) \mapsto \nabla_X\sigma, \end{equation}

such that

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(f\sigma) = \nabla_X(f)\sigma + f\nabla_X\sigma \end{equation}

for all $X\in \calx(M)$, $\sigma\in \Gamma(E)$, and $f\in C^\infty(M)$. In this equation, we define

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(f) = X(f). \end{equation}

Blah!

Theorem. Let $\nabla$ be a Koszul connection on a vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$.

  1. If $X$ and $Y$ are two vector fields on $M$ that agree at a point $p\in M$, then \begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_X\sigma)_p=(\nabla_Y\sigma)_p \end{equation} for all sections $\sigma \in \Gamma(E)$.
  2. If $\sigma$ and $\tau$ are two global sections of $E$ that coincide on an open neighborhood of a point $p\in M$, then \begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_X\sigma)_p = (\nabla_X\tau)_p \end{equation} for all vector fields $X\in \calx(M)$.

Blah!

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. We define a field of Koszul connections on $E$ (or simply a field of connections) to be a collection $\{\nabla^p\}_{p\in M}$ of $\bbr$-linear maps

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla^p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \cale_p \to E_p, \quad (v,\sigma) \mapsto \nabla^p_v \sigma, \end{equation}

such that

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla^p_v(f\sigma) = (\nabla_v^pf)\sigma_p + f(p) \nabla_v^p\sigma \end{equation}

for all $v\in T_p(M)$, $\sigma\in \cale_p$, and $f\in C^{\infty}_{M,p}$. In this equation, we define

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_v^pf) = df_p(v). \end{equation}

The field of connections is said to be smooth if, for all smooth vector fields $X\in \calx(M)$ and smooth sections $\sigma \in \cale(M)$, the induced section

\begin{equation}\notag p\mapsto \nabla_{X_p}^p \sigma \in E_p \end{equation}

is smooth.

Blah!

Theorem. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $N$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$, let $\alpha:M \to N$ be a smooth map, and let $\alpha^\ast E$ be the pullback bundle over $M$ with sheaf of sections $\alpha^\ast \cale$. If $\nabla$ is a connection on $E$, then there is a unique connection

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast\nabla: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \Gamma(\alpha^\ast E) \to \Gamma(\alpha^\ast E), \end{equation}

called the pullback connection, such that

\begin{equation}\label{defining-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^p_v(\alpha^\ast \sigma) = \nabla^q_w\sigma \end{equation}

for all $v\in T_p(M)$ and $\sigma \in \cale_q$, where $q = \alpha(p)$ and $w = \alpha_{\ast,p}(v)$.

Suppose that such a connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$ exists, satisfying \eqref{defining-eqn}. Then, since the natural map

\begin{equation}\notag \Theta_p : C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \cale_q \xrightarrow{\cong} (\alpha \cale)_p, \quad f \otimes \sigma \mapsto f \alpha^\ast \sigma, \end{equation}

is an isomorphism, the action of $\alpha^\ast \nabla^p_v$ on the stalk $(\alpha \cale)_p$ is determined by its action on (germs of) sections of the form $f\alpha^\ast \sigma$. But, by the Leibniz rule, we have

\begin{equation}\label{extend-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^p_v(f \alpha^\ast \sigma) = df_p(v) \sigma_q + f(p) \nabla^q_w\sigma \end{equation}

where $q = \alpha(p)$ and $w = \alpha_{\ast,p}(v)$. Thus, if the connection $\alpha^\ast \nabla$ exists, then it is uniquely determined by the equations \eqref{defining-eqn}.

To establish existence of the pullback connection, we shall construct it as a smooth field of connections. The first step is to consider the isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{first-eqn} T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr (\alpha^\ast \cale)_p \xrightarrow{\cong} T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \left(C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \cale_q \right) \end{equation}

induced by the natural map $\Theta_p$ above. Now, we have two maps

\begin{equation}\label{second-eqn} \left(T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr C^\infty_{M,p}\right) \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \cale_q \xrightarrow{d \otimes \dev_q} \bbr \otimes_{\bbr} E_q \xrightarrow{\cong} E_q \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\label{third-eqn} C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \left( T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr \cale_q \right) \xrightarrow{\id \otimes \alpha_{\alpha,p} \otimes \id} C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty_{N,q}} \left( T_q(N) \otimes_\bbr \cale_q \right) \xrightarrow{\dev_p \otimes \nabla^q} \bbr \otimes_\bbr E_q \xrightarrow{\cong} E_q. \end{equation}

Notice that the domains of \eqref{second-eqn} and \eqref{third-eqn} are naturally isomorphic to the codomain of the isomorphism \eqref{first-eqn} via natural associativity and commutativity isomorphisms of the tensor product. Therefore, we may define

\begin{equation}\label{field-eqn} \alpha^\ast\nabla^p: T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr (\alpha^\ast \cale)_p \to (\alpha^\ast E)_p = E_q \end{equation}

to be the isomorphism \eqref{first-eqn} followed by the sum of the two maps \eqref{second-eqn} and \eqref{third-eqn}. As the reader may easily check, the resulting map $\alpha^\ast \nabla^p_v$ satisfies the Leibniz rule for each $v\in T_p(M)$, and thus we have obtained a field of connections on the pullback $\alpha^\ast E$. If you trace through this argument, you’ll notice that we have essentially defined the pullback connection by the formula \eqref{extend-eqn}.

All that remains is to show that the field of connections given by \eqref{field-eqn} is smooth. For this, let’s suppose that the vector bundle $E$ and the tangent bundle $T(N)$ both trivialize over the open set $V\subseteq N$. Let

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \cale(V) \end{equation}

be a frame of $E$, and let

\begin{equation}\notag \bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n \in \calx(V) \end{equation}

be the coordinate frame.

Connections on direct sums

Forms and fields

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold, $k\geq 1$ an integer, and write $F(M)$ for the commutative $\bbr$-algebra of all real-valued functions $f:M \to \bbr$, not necessarily smooth. We define a $k$-form on $M$ to be a $C^\infty(M)$-linear function

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \underbrace{\calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \cdots \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M)}_{\text{$k$ factors}} \to F(M). \end{equation}

Here, the algebra $F(M)$ is viewed as a $C^\infty(M)$-module by restricting scalars along the inclusion map $C^\infty(M) \to F(M)$ of $\bbr$-algebras.

  1. The $k$-form $\omega$ is said to be symmetric if \begin{equation}\notag \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_i,\ldots,X_j,\ldots,X_k) = \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_j,\ldots,X_i,\ldots,X_k) \end{equation} for all $X_1,\ldots,X_k\in \calx(M)$ and $1\leq i < j \leq k$.
  2. The $k$-form $\omega$ is said to be alternating (or anti-symmetric) if \begin{equation}\notag \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_i,\ldots,X_j,\ldots,X_k) = -\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_j,\ldots,X_i,\ldots,X_k) \end{equation} for all $X_1,\ldots,X_k\in \calx(M)$ and $1\leq i < j \leq k$.
  3. The $k$-form $\omega$ is said to be smooth if its image lands in the subalgebra $C^\infty(M)$ of $F(M)$.

Given two $k$-forms $\omega,\eta$ and a scalar $c\in \bbr$, we define the sum $k$-form $\omega+\eta$ and the product $k$-form $c\omega$ via the following formulas:

\begin{equation}\notag (\omega+\eta)(X_1,\ldots,X_k) = \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k) + \eta(X_1,\ldots,X_k) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag (c\omega)(X_1,\ldots,X_k) = c\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k). \end{equation}

Forms of certain types naturally form $\bbr$-vector spaces with respect to these operations:

  1. We will write $T^{\text{$k$-form}}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all $k$-forms on $M$. The subspace of all smooth $k$-forms will be written as $T^{\text{$k$-form}}(M)$.
  2. We will write $S^{\text{$k$-form}}_\text{rough}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all symmetric $k$-forms on $M$. The subspace of all smooth symmetric $k$-forms will be written as $S^{\text{$k$-form}}(M)$.
  3. We will write $A^{\text{$k$-form}}_\text{rough}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all alternating $k$-forms on $M$. The subspace of all smooth alternating $k$-forms will be written as $A^{\text{$k$-form}}(M)$.

Exercise. Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. Given a $k$-form $\omega \in T^\text{$k$-form}_\text{rough}(M)$ and a function $f\in F(M)$, define their product, denoted $f\omega$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (f\omega)(X_1,\ldots,X_k)(p) = f(p) \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)(p). \end{equation}

  1. Prove that $T^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ is an $F(M)$-module with respect to the above product, and that $S^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ and $A^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ are $F(M)$-submodules.
  2. Prove that $T^\text{$k$-form}(M)$ is a $C^\infty(M)$-module with respect to the above product, and that $S^\text{$k$-form}(M)$ and $A^\text{$k$-form}(M)$ are $C^\infty(M)$-submodules.

The usage of the tensor product $\otimes_{C^\infty(M)}$ in the definition of a $k$-form $\omega$ is just a cute way of saying that $\omega$ is (equivalently) a $C^\infty(M)$-multilinear function

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \underbrace{\calx(M) \times \cdots \times \calx(M)}_{\text{$k$ factors}} \to F(M). \end{equation}

Indeed, we know that multilinearity on a cartesian product is the same as linearity on a tensor product.

Notation. Since iterated tensor products will appear so often in this section, it will be convenient to define

\begin{equation}\notag V^{\otimes_A k} = \underbrace{V \otimes_A \cdots \otimes_A V}_{\text{$k$ factors}} \end{equation}

for all integers $k\geq 1$ and all modules $V$ over a commutative ring $A$.

The main result in this section will be the equivalence of $k$-forms on a manifold with certain types of objects that we call $k$-fields. We define these latter objects below, using the definition of $k$-forms as a template. Notice, however, that we do not define smoothness of $k$-fields. We will address smoothness later.

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. A $k$-field on $M$ is a collection $\sigma = \{\sigma_p\}_{p\in M}$ of $\bbr$-linear maps

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p : T_p(M)^{\otimes_\bbr k} \to \bbr, \end{equation}

one for each point $p\in M$.

  1. The $k$-field $\sigma$ is said to be symmetric if \begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p(v_1,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_k) = \sigma_p(v_1,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_k) \end{equation} for all $v_1,\ldots,v_k\in T_p(M)$, all $p\in M$, and $1\leq i < j \leq k$.
  2. The $k$-field $\sigma$ is said to be alternating (or anti-symmetric) if \begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p(v_1,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_k) = -\sigma_p(v_1,\ldots,v_j,\ldots,v_i,\ldots,v_k) \end{equation} for all $v_1,\ldots,v_k\in T_p(M)$, all $p\in M$, and $1\leq i < j \leq k$.

Given two $k$-fields $\sigma,\tau$ and a scalar $c\in \bbr$, we define the sum $k$-field $\sigma + \tau$ and the product $k$-field $c\sigma$ via the following formulas:

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma + \tau = \{\sigma_p + \tau_p\} \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag c\sigma = \{c\sigma_p\}, \end{equation}

where $\sigma_p+\tau_p$ and $c\sigma_p$ are the evident pointwise sum and product. Fields of certain types naturally form $\bbr$-vector spaces with respect to these operations:

  1. We will write $T^{\text{$k$-field}}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all $k$-fields on $M$.
  2. We will write $S^{\text{$k$-field}}_\text{rough}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all symmetric $k$-fields on $M$.
  3. We will write $A^{\text{$k$-field}}_\text{rough}(M)$ for the $\bbr$-vector space of all alternating $k$-fields on $M$.

Exercise. Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. Given a $k$-field $\sigma \in T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(M)$ and a function $f\in F(M)$, define their product, denoted $f\sigma$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag f\sigma = \{f\sigma_p\}, \end{equation}

where

\begin{equation}\notag (f\sigma_p)(v_1,\ldots,v_k) \defeq f(p)\sigma(v_1,\ldots,v_k). \end{equation}

Prove that $T^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ is an $F(M)$-module with respect to the above product, and that $S^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ and $A^\text{$k$-form}_{\text{rough}}(M)$ are $F(M)$-submodules.

Fields have one distinct advantage over forms, in that the former have obvious restriction maps yielding a sheaf structure. Before we describe them, you should notice that, just as $C^\infty_M$ is a sheaf, so too is the assignment $U \mapsto F(U)$ which maps an open subset of $M$ to the collection of all real-valued functions on $U$. This latter sheaf is a sheaf of commutative $\bbr$-algebras on $M$, and it will be denoted $F_M$.

Now, let $U$ and $V$ be two nonempty open subsets of $M$ with $U\subseteq V$ and define the $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \rho^V_U : T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(V) \to T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U) \end{equation}

by setting

\begin{equation}\notag \rho^V_U(\sigma) = \{ \sigma_p\}_{p\in U}. \end{equation}

This is called a restriction map.

Exercise. Let $M$ be a manifold.

  1. Prove that the restriction map $\rho^V_U$ is $F(V)$-linear, where $T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U)$ is given an $F(V)$-module structure by restriction of scalars along the evident restriction map $F(V)\to F(U)$.
  2. If $U$ is an open set in $M$, prove that the restriction map $\rho^U_U$ is the identity map on $\calx(U)$.
  3. If $U\subseteq V \subseteq W$ are open subsets of $M$, prove that \begin{equation}\notag \rho^W_U = \rho^{V}_U\circ \rho^W_V. \end{equation}
  4. Suppose $U$ is an open subset of $M$, and that $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ is an open cover of $U$. Prove that if $\sigma,\tau\in T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U)$ and $\rho^U_{U_i}(\sigma) = \rho^U_{U_i}(\tau)$ for all $i\in I$, then $\sigma=\tau$ as $k$-fields.
  5. Continuing with the open cover $\{U_i\}$ from the previous part, suppose that for each $i\in I$ we have a $k$-field $\sigma_i \in T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U_i)$. Suppose further that the $\sigma_i$’s agree on overlaps, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag \rho^{U_i}_{U_i\cap U_j}(X_i) = \rho^{U_j}_{U_i\cap U_j} (X_j) \end{equation} for all $i,j\in I$. Prove that there is a (necessarily unique!) $k$-field $\sigma\in T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U)$ such that $\rho^U_{U_i}(\sigma) = \sigma_i$ for all $i\in I$.

The exercise thus shows that the assignment $U \mapsto T^\text{$k$-field}_\text{rough}(U)$ is a sheaf of $F_M$-modules.

Thus, the basic situation at hand is the relationship between the following two sets:

\begin{equation}\notag \left\{ \text{fields of $k$-multilinear forms on $M$} \right\} \quad \leftrightarrow \quad \left\{\text{$k$-forms on $M$}\right\}. \end{equation}

As we just explained, it is easy to move from left to right, but the reverse mechanism that produces a field of $k$-multilinear forms from a $k$-form $\omega$ is not so obvious. Our goal in the rest of this section is to understand this mechanism. To give away the punchline, it will turn out that the key feature of a $k$-form that makes this process work is the purely algebraic property of $C^\infty(M)$-linearity.

We thus begin with a definition where we only assume $\bbr$-linearity, and not $C^\infty(M)$-linearity.

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. We shall say that an $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \calx(M)^{\otimes_{C^\infty(M)} k} \to F(M) \end{equation}

induces a field if

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) = \left[\omega(Y_1,\ldots,Y_k)\right](p) \end{equation}

whenever the $X_i$’s and $Y_i$’s are vector fields such that $X_i|_p = Y_i|_p$ for each $i$.

The reason for the terminology “induces a field” is given here:

Theorem. Let $M$ be a manifold. If an $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \calx(M)^{\otimes_{C^\infty(M)} k} \to F(M) \end{equation}

induces a field, then $\omega$ determines a unique field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$ of $k$-multilinear forms

\begin{equation}\notag \omega_p : T_p(M)^{\otimes_\bbr k} \to \bbr, \end{equation}

by the formula

\begin{equation}\label{mirror-eqn} \omega_p(v_1,\ldots,v_k) = \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p), \end{equation}

where the $X_i$’s are any vector fields such that $X_i|_p=v_i$ for each $i$.

If such a field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$ exists, then $\omega$ may be recovered through the equation

\begin{equation}\label{tile-eqn} \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) = \omega_p(X_1|_p,\ldots,X_1|_p). \end{equation}

Thus, the field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$, if it exists, is unique. But for existence, we recall that such vector fields $X_1,\ldots,X_k$ as in \eqref{mirror-eqn} always exists. It then follows immediately from the “induces a field” property that each $\omega_p$ is well-defined. Q.E.D.

Now, the fascinating bit is that the “field inducing” property of an $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \calx(M)^{\otimes_{C^\infty(M)} k} \to F(M) \end{equation}

has an entirely algebraic characterization, encapsulated in the following result. The name of this result is borrowed from Besse’s book ([^Besse2008]), though it is present in some form or another in virtually all textbooks on differential geometry. It is stated here as a “first version”; a more general version will be given later when we talk about vector bundles.

Fundamental Lemma of Differential Geometry (First version). Let $M$ be a manifold. An $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \calx(M)^{\otimes_{C^\infty(M)} k} \to F(M) \end{equation}

induces a field if and only if it is $C^\infty(M)$-linear.

The “only if” portion of the proof is trivial, in view of the relationship \eqref{tile-eqn} between $\omega$ and the induced field $\{\omega_p\}$. The “if” portion of the proof is more interesting. I will leave it to you to show that it suffices to prove

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) = 0, \end{equation}

if we have $X_j|_p=0$ for (at least) one index $j$. To prove this, fix such an index $j$ and choose local coordinates $x^1,\ldots,x^n$ on an open neighborhood $U$ near $p$, and let $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ be the corresponding local frame. Then, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag X_j = X^i_j \bd_i, \end{equation}

with summation over $i$ implied. By shrinking $U$ if needed, we may choose a bump function $h\in C^\infty(M)$ with support in $U$ and $h(p)=1$. But then each $hX^i_j$ may be considered a smooth function on all of $M$, and likewise each $h\bd_i$ is a smooth vector field on $M$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag h^2 \omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k) = \omega(X_1,\ldots,h^2 X_j,\ldots,X_k) = hX^i_j \omega(X_1,\ldots, h \bd_i,\ldots,X_k). \end{equation}

Hence

\begin{align} \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) &= h(p)^2 \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) \notag \\ &= h(p)X^i_j(p) \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,h \bd_i,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) \notag \\ &= 0, \notag \end{align}

since $X^i_j(p)=0$ for each $i$. Q.E.D.

Let’s now summarize the situation in the following theorem:

Theorem (Forms and fields are equivalent). Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. Then there is a bijective correspondence between the set of all $k$-forms $\omega$ on $M$ and the set of all fields $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$ of $k$-multlinear forms

\begin{equation}\notag \omega_p: T_p(M)^{\otimes_\bbr k} \to \bbr. \end{equation}

Indeed:

  1. Given the $k$-form $\omega$, the field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$ is obtained by defining \begin{equation}\notag \omega_p(v_1,\ldots,v_k) = \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p), \end{equation} where the $X_i$’s are any vector fields such that $X_i|_p=v_i$ for each $i$.
  2. Given field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$, the $k$-form $\omega$ is obtained by defining \begin{equation}\notag \left[\omega(X_1,\ldots,X_k)\right](p) = \omega_p(X_1|_p,\ldots,X_k|_p). \end{equation}

In view of this equivalence, we may define properties of a $k$-form in terms of properties of the associated field, and vice versa. We use this strategy in the following set of definitions:

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold and $k\geq 1$ an integer. A field $\{\omega_p\}_{p\in M}$ of $k$-multlinear forms on $M$ is called smooth, or symmetric, or alternating, if the associated $k$-form $\omega$ is smooth, or symmetric, or alternating.

In the other direction, we can also give important definitions of forms in terms of their associated fields. In the following definition, we focus on properties of symmetric $2$-forms, which are central to riemannian geometry, and are traditionally denoted by $g$ rather than $\omega$.

Definition. Let

\begin{equation}\notag g: \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \to C^\infty(M) \end{equation}

be a symmetric $2$-form on a manifold $M$ with associated field of symmetric bilinear forms

\begin{equation}\notag g_p: T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr T_p(M) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

  1. We shall say $g$ is a scalar product if each $g_p$ is non-degenerate.
  2. We shall say $g$ is an inner product if each $g_p$ is positive definite.

(Pseudo-)riemannian manifolds

Definition. A pseudo-riemannian manifold is a pair $(M,g)$ consisting of a manifold $M$ and a smooth scalar product

\begin{equation}\notag g: \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \to C^\infty(M). \end{equation}

If $g$ is an inner product, then $(M,g)$ will be called a riemannian manifold. In the pseudo-riemannian case, the scalar product $g$ is called the pseudo-metric (or pseudo-metric tensor) of the manifold, while it is called the metric (or metric tensor) in the riemannian case. When we do not need to mention the metric explicitly, we shall refer to just $M$ as a (pseudo-)riemannian manifold.

By virtue of the fact that our manifolds come embedded in euclidean space, they all have riemannian structures with particularly nice features. The existence of pseudo-riemannian structures on embedded submanifolds is a more delicate question, which we will address in the next section when we look at special examples of pseudo-riemannian manifolds called lorentzian manifolds.

To obtain the riemannian structure, suppose that $M$ is a manifold embedded in $\bbr^s$. The standard euclidean inner product on $\bbr^s$ restricts to a field of inner products

\begin{equation}\notag g_p: T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr T_p(M) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

Thus, an inner product

\begin{equation}\notag g: \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \to F(M) \end{equation}

on the manifold is obtained, but in order to show that we have a riemannian structure, we need to show that the field $\{g_p\}_{p\in M}$ is smooth, which amounts to showing that the image of $g$ lands in the subalgebra $C^\infty(M)$ of $F(M)$.

Theorem (Embedded manifolds are riemannian). Let $M$ be a manifold. The field of positive definite inner products $g_p$ that $M$ inherits from the ambient euclidean space is smooth.

Given two vector fields $X,Y\in \calx(M)$, to prove the theorem it will suffice to show that the function

\begin{equation}\notag p \mapsto g(X,Y)(p) \defeq g_p(X_p,Y_p)_p \end{equation}

is smooth on every open parametrizable subset $U$. To do this, we first let $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ be a coordinate local frame over $U$ and write

\begin{equation}\label{metric-coeff-eqn} g_{ij}(p) = g_p(\bd_i|_p,\bd_j|_p) \end{equation}

for each $p\in U$. If the corresponding local parametrization on $U$ is $\phi:\bbr^n \to U$ with $\phi(x)=p$, then, in fact, we have

\begin{equation}\notag g_{ij}(p) = \sum_{l=1}^n\frac{\bd \phi^l}{\bd x^i}(x)\frac{\bd \phi^l}{\bd x^j}(x), \end{equation}

which shows that $p\mapsto g_{ij}(p)$ is a smooth function on $U$. But then, we have

\begin{equation}\notag g(X,Y)(p)= X^i(p)Y^j(p)g_{ij}(p) \end{equation}

for all $p\in U$, which shows that $p\mapsto g(X,Y)(p)$ is smooth on $U$. Q.E.D.

The functions \eqref{metric-coeff-eqn} in the proof are important enough to have their own name:

Definition. Let $(M,g)$ be a riemannian manifold and let $U$ be an open parametrizable subset with coordinate local frame $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$. For $1\leq i,j\leq n$, the smooth functions

\begin{equation}\notag g_{ij}: U \to \bbr, \quad g_{ij}(p) = g_p(\bd_i|_p,\bd_j|_p) \end{equation}

are called the metric coefficients (over $U$).

The notation $g_{ij}$ for the metric coefficients is ambiguous, since it does not specify the identity of the open parametrizable subset $U$.

It is a common practice to write the metric coefficients in a matrix. For example, the metric coefficients of the standard euclidean product on $\bbr^3$ would be represented as

\begin{equation}\notag g = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} & g_{13} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} & g_{23} \\ g_{31} & g_{32} & g_{33} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

Of course, in this case, the metric coefficient functions $g_{ij}$ are defined on the whole of $\bbr^3$.

Riemannian, or no? As we have just seen, all of our (embedded) manifolds are riemannian, inheriting a metric from the ambient euclidean inner products. In fact, by utilizing a construction with partitions of unity, it may be shown that all smooth manifolds are riemannian, regardless of whether they are embedded (from the outset) as a submanifold of euclidean space.

Thus, in the rest of this post, when we write “Let $M$ be a riemannian manifold…” in the hypotheses of theorems and definitions, we could just as well have written “Let $M$ be a manifold…”. Hypothesizing the existence of a riemannian metric on a manifold is, in view of the results just cited, no extra condition on the manifold.

However, even though all of our manifolds inherit riemannian structures from the ambient euclidean spaces, it will be important for us to note which results below depend only on the abstract properties of the metric (e.g., that it induces inner products on tangent space), and not on the specific construction of the metric from the ambient inner products. To help identify these results, we will distinguish between two types of riemannian manifolds:

  1. When the embedded manifold $M$ inherits its riemannian structure from the ambient euclidean space, we will refer to $M$ as an embedded riemannian manifold.
  2. Otherwise, a riemannian manifold remains defined as above, where we assume nothing about the provenance of the metric, and view it only as an abstract $C^\infty(M)$-linear map that induces a smooth field of inner products.

The distinction at play is whether a riemannian manifold $M$ is embedded in euclidean space merely as a smooth manifold, or whether it is embedded as a riemannian manifold. Since we are still following the definition of manifold from Guillemin and Pollack’s book, all of our riemannian manifolds are embedded as smooth manifolds, but their metrics may not be inherited from the ambient euclidean space, which means they may not be embedded riemannian manifolds.

Gradients

The presence of the metric on a riemannian manifold $M$ allows us to define the gradient vector fields of smooth functions, which are likely very familiar from your study of multivariable calculus. More generally, the metric allows us to identify vector fields with certain objects called $1$-forms, that we will study later.

To prepare the way, we first recall the metric coefficient functions $p\mapsto g_{ij}(p)$ defined in the previous section, which are smooth functions defined on open parametrizable subsets $U$ of the manifold $M$. Fixing a point $p\in U$, the induced inner product

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{-,-}_p : T_p(M) \otimes_\bbr T_p(M) \to \bbr \end{equation}

is nondegenerate, and hence induces a linear isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{iso-eqn} T_p(M) \xrightarrow{\cong} T_p^\ast(M) , \quad v\mapsto \ang{v,-}, \end{equation}

between the tangent and cotangent space at $p$. If $x^1,\ldots,x^n$ are local coordinates on $U$ and $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ is the associated coordinate local frame, then one may easily check that the matrix of the isomorphism \eqref{iso-eqn} with respect to the bases

\begin{equation}\notag \bd_1|_p,\ldots,\bd_n|_p \in T_p(M) \quad \text{and} \quad dx^1_p,\ldots,dx^n_p\in T_p^\ast(M) \end{equation}

is exactly $[g_{ij}(p)]_{ij}$. The matrix of the inverse of \eqref{iso-eqn} with respect to these same bases is conventionally denoted $[g^{ij}(p)]_{ij}$, with the subscripts simply moving upstairs to become superscripts. Since inversion of a matrix is a smooth process, the functions $p\mapsto g^{ij}(p)$ are smooth on $U$. The defining relationship between the $g_{ij}(p)$’s and the $g^{ij}(p)$’s is encapsulated in the equality

\begin{equation}\notag g^{ik}(p) g_{kj}(p) = \delta^i_j, \end{equation}

where $\delta^{i}_j$ is the Kronecker delta, equal to $1$ if $i=j$, and $0$ otherwise. Furthermore, both the $g_{ij}(p)$’s and $g^{ij}(p)$’s are symmetric, meaning that

\begin{equation}\notag g_{ij}(p) = g_{ji}(p) \quad \text{and} \quad g^{ij}(p) = g^{ji}(p) \end{equation}

for all $i,j$.

Now, recall that if $f\in C^\infty(M)$ is a smooth function, then at every point $p\in M$ we have the differential $df_p \in T_p^\ast(M)$.

Theorem (The riemannian gradient). Let $f$ be a smooth function on a riemannian manifold $M$. There is a unique smooth vector field $\nabla f$ on $M$ such that

\begin{equation}\label{form1-eqn} \ang{(\nabla f)_p, v}_p = df_p(v), \end{equation}

for all $p\in M$ and all $v\in T_p(M)$. In particular, if $x^1,\ldots,x^n$ are local coordinates near $p$ and $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ is the associated coordinate local frame, then

\begin{equation}\label{form2-eqn} (\nabla f)_p = g^{ij}(p) \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j}(p) \bd_i|_p. \end{equation}

The vector field $\nabla f$ is called the gradient vector field of $f$.

If it exists, uniqueness of $\nabla f$ follows immediately from the nondegeneracy of the inner products $\ang{-,-}_p$. For existence, we simply show that with the formula \eqref{form2-eqn}, the equation \eqref{form1-eqn} holds:

\begin{equation}\notag \left\langle g^{ij}(p) \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j}(p) \bd_i|_p,v \right\rangle_p = g_{ki}(p)g^{ij}(p) \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j}(p) v^k = \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j}(p) v^j = df_p(v). \end{equation}

Now, to see that $\nabla f$ is a smooth vector field, we simply appeal to the formula \eqref{form2-eqn} and note that the functions $g^{ij}$ and the partial derivatives are smooth. Q.E.D.

Theorem (Inner products with gradients). Let $f$ be a smooth function on a riemannian manifold $M$. If we interpret a smooth vector field $X\in \calx(M)$ as a derivation on $C^\infty(M)$, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag X(f) = \ang{\nabla f, X} \end{equation}

as functions in $C^\infty(M)$.

For proof, simply note that

\begin{equation}\notag \left[X(f)\right](p) = df_p(X_p) = \ang{(\nabla f)_p, X_p}_p = \ang{\nabla f, X}(p) \end{equation}

for all $p\in M$. Thus $X(f) = \ang{\nabla f, X}$, as desired. Q.E.D.

Levi-Civita connections

Given a riemannian manifold $M$, we already have a way to differentiate a smooth function $f$ “in the direction” of a vector field $X$. Indeed, such a derivative is simply the smooth function

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X f \defeq \ang{\nabla f, X} \in C^\infty(M), \end{equation}

where $\nabla f$ is the gradient vector field of $f$ constructed in the previous section. The goal in this section is to extend this derivative operator $\nabla_X$ to vector fields on $M$, in order to make sense of something like $\nabla_X Y$, where both $X,Y\in \calx(M)$.

First, suppose that $M$ is an embedded riemannian manifold, inheriting its metric from the ambient euclidean space $\bbr^s$. In addition to giving us a riemannian structure on $M$, the ambient euclidean inner product also enables us to define the orthogonal complement of the tangent space $T_p(M)$ by setting

\begin{equation}\notag \nu_p(M) = \left\{ w\in \bbr^s : \ang{v,w} =0, \ \forall v\in T_p(M)\right\}. \end{equation}

We then have the fundamental direct sum decomposition

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr^s = T_p(M) \oplus \nu_p(M), \end{equation}

that yields a natural surjective linear map $\bbr^s \to T_p(M)$ which projects a vector $w\in \bbr^s$ to its (unique!) component in $T_p(M)$. We shall denote this latter projection by $w|_{T_p(M)}$.

We will use these orthogonal projections to define the extension of $\nabla_X$ to an operator that acts on vector fields:

Definition. Let $M$ be an embedded riemannian manifold. The Levi-Civita connection on $M$ (or simply the connection on $M$) is the $\bbr$-linear function $\nabla$ which acts on both $\calx(M)$ and $C^\infty(M)$ as follows:

  1. Its action on vector fields yields a function \begin{equation}\notag \nabla: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto \nabla_XY, \end{equation} given by \begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_XY)_p = Y_{\ast,p}(X_p)|_{T_p(M)}, \end{equation} for $p\in M$.

  2. Its action on functions yields a function \begin{equation}\notag \nabla: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr C^\infty(M) \to C^\infty(M), \quad (X,f) \mapsto \nabla_Xf, \end{equation} given by \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_Xf = \ang{\nabla f, X}, \end{equation} for $f\in C^\infty(M)$.

The vector field $\nabla_X Y$ and the function $\nabla_X f$ are called the covariant derivatives (in the direction of $X$) of $Y$ and $f$, respectively.

The covariant derivative $\nabla_XY$ is computed at a point $p\in M$ by first computing the directional derivative of $Y$ in the direction of $X_p$; then, this directional derivative is “projected” to the manifold $M$, and thus $\nabla_XY$ is exactly the component of this directional derivative that the manifold $M$ “sees.”

I mentioned that the Levi-Civita connection is $\bbr$-bilinear without proof. In fact, the connection has several other important properties which are listed in the next exercise.

Exercise. Let $M$ be an embedded riemannian manifold with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$.

  1. Prove that the connection is actually $\bbr$-bilinear.
  2. Prove that the connection is $C^\infty(M)$-linear in the first argument, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_{fX}Y = f\nabla_XY \end{equation} and \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_{fX}g = f \nabla_Xg \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$ and all $f,g\in C^\infty(M)$.
  3. Prove that the connection is a “derivation” in the second argument, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(fY) = (\nabla_Xf)Y + f \nabla_XY \end{equation} and \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(fg) = (\nabla_Xf)g + f \nabla_Xg \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$ and all $f,g\in C^\infty(M)$.

Since a Levi-Civita connection is $C^\infty(M)$-linear in the first argument, it induces a field! Precisely:

Theorem (Connections are local objects in the first argument). Let $M$ be an embedded riemannian manifold with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$. Fixing a vector field $Y\in \calx(M)$, for each point $p\in M$ there is a unique $\bbr$-linear function

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla Y: T_p(M) \to \bbr, \quad v \mapsto \nabla_v Y, \end{equation}

such that

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_vY = \nabla_XY, \end{equation}

where $X$ is a vector field in $\calx(M)$ with $X_p=v$.

It is convenient to introduce the so-called Christoffel symbols of the Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$ on an $n$-dimensional embedded riemannian manifold $M$. These are collections of numbers that completely determine the connection on an open parametrizable subset $U$. Given a coordinate local frame $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ on $U$, the Christoffel symbols, denoted $\Gamma^k_{ij}$, are defined via the equation

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_{\bd_i}\bd_j = \Gamma^k_{ij} \bd_k. \end{equation}

As with many objects in differential geometry, notice that the Christoffel symbols depend on the choice of local coordinates, though this dependence is not visible in the notation $\Gamma^k_{ij}$.

Now, suppose we are given two vector fields $X,Y\in \calx(M)$. On the open set $U$, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag X = X^i\bd_i \quad \text{and} \quad Y = Y^j \bd_j, \end{equation}

where $X^i,Y^j \in C^\infty(U)$ are the (local) component functions of $X$ and $Y$. Hence, as you may easily check, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_XY = \left( \left\langle \nabla Y^k,X \right\rangle + X^i Y^j \Gamma^k_{ij} \right) \bd_k. \end{equation}

In particular, if $M=\bbr^n$, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_XY = \left\langle \nabla Y^k,X \right\rangle \bd_k, \end{equation}

since $\Gamma^k_{ij}=0$ in this case. Therefore, the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ may be thought of as “correction terms” that quantify the (local, coordinate-dependent) discrepancy between the Levi-Civita connection on an arbitrary manifold and the Levi-Civita connection on “flat” euclidean space.

It is instructive to study the Levi-Civita connection for some simple manifolds. For example, let’s suppose that we want to understand the vanishing condition $\nabla_XY=0$ in the case that $X$ and $Y$ are vector fields on the unit circle $M=S^1$. We may parametrize an open neighborhood of a point $p\in S^1$ via the map

\begin{equation}\notag \theta \mapsto (\cos{\theta},\sin{\theta}), \end{equation}

suitably restricted to an open interval of $\theta$’s. In the local coordinate $\theta$, the vector fields $X$ and $Y$ take the form

\begin{equation}\notag X_\theta = f(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}) \quad \text{and} \quad Y_\theta = g(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}), \end{equation}

for some pair of smooth functions $f(\theta)$ and $g(\theta)$. With respect to the trivialization

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha: S^1 \times \bbr \xrightarrow{\cong} T(S^1), \quad (x,y,r) \mapsto (x,y,-yr, xr), \end{equation}

that we discovered in the post on tangent bundles, the functions $f(\theta)$ and $g(\theta)$ measure the height (above or below) of the vector fields $X$ and $Y$ above the zero section:

Now, we compute

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{dY_\theta}{d\theta} = g’(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}) - g(\theta)(\cos{\theta},\sin{\theta}), \end{equation}

so that if $p$ has local coordinate $\theta$, we get that

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_XY)_p = f(\theta)g’(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}). \end{equation}

In particular, if we take $f(\theta)=1$ for all $\theta$, so that $X$ is a “uniform” vector field of unit tangent vectors, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_XY)_p = g’(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}). \end{equation}

This shows that the covariant derivative $\nabla_XY$ measures the “vertical” rate of change of the vector field $Y$, when the tangent bundle $T(S^1)$ is identified with the cylinder $S^1\times \bbr$ as drawn above. In particular, if $\nabla_XY=0$, then $Y$ must be a “horizontal” section of the tangent bundle.

Back to the general theory. An embedded riemannian manifold $M$ thus has two structures: it has its metric $\ang{-,-}$, inherited from the ambient euclidean space, and it has its Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$. These two structures fit together coherently, in the sense of the following theorem, which states that the covariant derivative of an inner product may computed via the familiar “product rule”.

Theorem (Compatibility with the metric). Let $M$ be an embedded riemannian manifold with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$, and let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be smooth vectors fields on $M$. Then the connection is compatible with the metric, in the sense that \begin{equation}\label{compat-eqn} \nabla_X \ang{Y,Z} = \ang{\nabla_XY,Z} + \ang{Y,\nabla_XZ}. \end{equation}

In fact, equation \eqref{compat-eqn} is a consequence of the equation \begin{equation}\notag \ang{Y,Z}_{\ast,p}(v) = \ang{Y_{\ast,p}(v),Z_p} + \ang{Y_p, Z_{\ast,p}(v)} \end{equation} for all points $p\in M$ and all tangent vectors $v\in T_p(M)$. Here, we recall that $\ang{Y,Z}$ is a vector field, interpreted as a smooth map from $M$ to the ambient euclidean space, and hence the pushforward $\ang{Y,Z}_{\ast,p}$ makes sense. On the right-hand side of the equation, however, the angle brackets $\ang{-,-}$ denote the euclidean inner product on the ambient space. For extra practice, I suggest that you establish this latter equation on your own, taking extra care to unwind the meaning of all the symbols.

Abstract connections and the Fundamental Theorem of Riemannian Geometry

In order to state the important theorem in the name of this section, we need a specific enrichment of structure on the module $\calx(M)$ of smooth vector fields on a manifold $M$. This structure is a way to “multiply” vector fields:

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold. The Lie bracket on $M$ is the $\bbr$-bilinear function

\begin{equation}\notag [-,-]: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto [X,Y] \end{equation}

given by

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]_p = X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) - Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) \end{equation}

for $p\in M$.

In this definition, we are considering smooth vector fields to be smooth maps to the ambient euclidean space in which $M$ is embedded. If we equip $M$ with its Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$, then the Lie bracket $[X,Y]$ appears to be the difference $\nabla_YX - \nabla_XY$, but the orthogonal projections onto the tangent spaces are missing. In fact, you may have wondered if I had forgotten these projections in the definition of the Lie bracket, because there is no reason to believe (at the outset) that the difference

\begin{equation}\notag X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) - Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) \end{equation}

is actually a tangent vector in $T_p(M)$. However, it actually is in the tangent space! No orthogonal projections are needed!

One way to prove this is to give local coordinate descriptions of the Lie bracket:

Theorem (Lie brackets in coordinates). Let $X$ and $Y$ be two smooth vector fields on a manifold $M$. If $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ is the coordinate local frame on an open parametrizable subset $U$ with local coordinates $x^1,\ldots,x^n$, and if

\begin{equation}\notag X = X^i \bd_i \quad \text{and} \quad Y = Y^i \bd_i \end{equation}

on $U$, then

\begin{equation}\label{coord-der-eqn} [X,Y] = \left(Y^i \frac{\bd X^j}{\bd x^i} - X^i \frac{\bd Y^j}{\bd x^i} \right) \bd_j \end{equation}

on $U$. In particular, the Lie bracket $[X,Y]$ is a smooth vector field on $M$.

To prove the theorem, we suppose $M$ is embedded in $\bbr^s$. Then:

  1. We let $\phi: \bbr^n \to U$ be the local parametrization corresponding to the local coordinates $x^1,\ldots,x^n$.
  2. We let $t^1,\ldots,t^n$ be the standard global euclidean coordinates on $\bbr^n$.
  3. We let $u_1,\ldots,u_n$ be the standard ordered basis of $\bbr^n$.
  4. We let $v_1,\ldots,v_s$ be the standard ordered basis of $\bbr^s$.

Now, as you may easily check, we have

\begin{equation}\notag X\circ \phi = a^j \frac{ \bd \phi^k}{\bd t^j} v_k \quad \text{and} \quad Y\circ \phi = b^j \frac{ \bd \phi^k}{\bd t^j} v_k \end{equation}

where $a^j = X^j \circ \phi$ and $b^j = Y^j \circ \phi$. Given $p\in U$ with $\phi(t)=p$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \phi_{\ast,t}(a^i(t)u_i) = X_p \quad \text{and} \quad \phi_{\ast,t}(b^i(t)u_i) = Y_p. \end{equation}

Thus, by the Chain Rule, we get

\begin{equation}\label{sput1-eqn} X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) = b^i(t) \frac{\bd a^j}{\bd t^i}(t) \frac{\bd \phi^k}{\bd t^j}(t)v_k + a^j(t)b^i(t) \frac{\bd ^2 \phi^k}{\bd t^i \bd t^j}(t) v_k \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\label{sput2-eqn} Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) = a^i(t) \frac{\bd b^j}{\bd t^i}(t) \frac{\bd \phi^k}{\bd t^j}(t)v_k + a^j(t)b^i(t) \frac{\bd^2 \phi^k}{\bd t^j \bd t^i}(t) v_k. \end{equation}

But then

\begin{align} X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) - Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) &= \left(b^i(t) \frac{\bd a^j}{\bd t^i}(t) - a^i(t) \frac{\bd b^j}{\bd t^i}(t) \right)\frac{\bd \phi^k}{\bd t^j}(t)v_k\notag \\ &= \left(Y^i(p) \frac{\bd X^j}{\bd x^i}(p) - X^i(t) \frac{\bd Y^j}{\bd x^i}(p) \right)\bd_j|_p, \notag \end{align}

where we used equality of the second-order mixed partial derivatives in \eqref{sput1-eqn} and \eqref{sput2-eqn} in the first equality. Q.E.D.

The Lie bracket takes a particularly simple form when we identify the module $\calx(M)$ with the module

\begin{equation}\label{der-eqn} \Der_\bbr(C^\infty(M),C^\infty(M)) \end{equation}

of derivations on $C^\infty(M)$.

Definition. Suppose that we identify the module of smooth vector fields $\calx(M)$ on a manifold $M$ with the module of derivations \eqref{der-eqn}. We then define the algebraic Lie bracket of two vector fields $X$ and $Y$ to be the derivation

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]_\text{alg} \defeq (Y\circ X) - (X\circ Y). \end{equation}

While the individual composites $Y\circ X$ and $X\circ Y$ are not (in general) derivations, I will leave it to you to show that their difference $[X,Y]_\text{alg}$ actually is.

Theorem (Algebraic Lie brackets in coordinates). Let $X$ and $Y$ be two smooth vector fields on a manifold $M$, viewed as derivations of $C^\infty(M)$. If $\bd_1,\ldots,\bd_n$ is the coordinate local frame on an open parametrizable subset $U$ with local coordinates $x^1,\ldots,x^n$, and if

\begin{equation}\notag X = X^i\frac{\bd}{\bd x^i} \quad \text{and} \quad Y = Y^i \frac{\bd}{\bd x^i} \end{equation}

on $U$, then

\begin{equation}\label{coord-der2-eqn} [X,Y]_\text{alg} = \left(Y^i\frac{\bd X^j}{\bd x^i} - X^i\frac{\bd Y^j}{\bd x^i}\right) \frac{\bd }{\bd x^j} \end{equation}

on $U$.

The crucial fact used in the proof of the theorem on local coordinate descriptions of $[-,-]$ was that certain second-order mixed partial derivatives were equal. This fact is also used in the current proof. Indeed, given $f\in C^\infty(U)$, we have

\begin{align} Y(X(f)) &= Y \left(X^j \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j} \right) \notag \\ &= Y(X^j) \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j} + X^j Y\left(\frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j}\right) \notag \\ &= Y^i\frac{\bd X^j}{\bd x^i} \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j} + X^j Y^i \frac{\bd^2 f}{\bd x^i \bd x^j} \notag \end{align}

and similarly

\begin{equation}\notag X(Y(f)) = X^i\frac{\bd Y^j}{\bd x^i} \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^j} + X^j Y^i \frac{\bd^2 f}{\bd x^j \bd x^i}. \end{equation}

Thus, we have

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]_\text{alg} = \left(Y^i\frac{\bd X^j}{\bd x^i} - X^i\frac{\bd Y^j}{\bd x^i}\right) \frac{\bd }{\bd x^j} \end{equation}

over $U$, as desired. Q.E.D.

Conclusion. By comparing equations \eqref{coord-der-eqn} and \eqref{coord-der2-eqn} in the theorems, we see that it does not matter whether we view vector fields as sections of tangent bundles or derivations, the Lie brackets are the same (up to isomorphism).

Now, since no orthogonal projection is involved in the definition of $[X,Y]$, we can give geometric interpretations of the Lie bracket relatively easily. To make it even easier, we will suppose that the manifold $M$ is an open submanifold of euclidean space $\bbr^n$, so that the pushforwards in the definition

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]_p = X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) - Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) \end{equation}

may be computed as directional derivatives. In particular, if a point $p\in M$ is fixed, then these pushforwards measure the first-order infinitesimal changes in the vector fields, in the sense that

\begin{equation}\notag Y_{q} - Y_p = \dev Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) + O(\dev^2) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag X_{r} - X_p = \dev X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) + O(\dev^2), \end{equation}

where $q=p+\dev X_p$ and $r = p+\dev Y_p$. Now, the vectors on the left-hand sides of these equations (each scaled by $\dev$) may be placed into a figure of the form:

Thus, we see that the Lie bracket $[X,Y]$ at a point $p$ measures the second-order infinitesimal deviation from the evident parallelogram being “closed.” In other words, if $[X,Y]_p=0$, then the two blue dots in the upper right-hand corner of the figure coincide, at least up to second-order infinitesimals.

We already noted above one of the most important properties of the Levi-Civita connection on an embedded riemannian manifold. Another fundametal property follows almost from the very definition of the Lie bracket:

Theorem (Levi-Civita connections have vanishing torsion). Let $M$ be an embedded riemannian manifold with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$. Then $\nabla$ is torsion free, in the sense that

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_YX - \nabla_XY = [X,Y] \end{equation}

for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$.

From the compatibility and torsion-free properties of the Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$, a fundamental (local) relationship between $\nabla$ and the metric may be derived. First, given vector fields $X,Y,Z\in \calx(M)$, we have

\begin{align} \nabla_X \ang{Y,Z} &= \ang{\nabla_XY,Z} + \ang{Y,\nabla_XZ} \notag \\ \nabla_Y \ang{Z,X} &= \ang{\nabla_YZ,X} + \ang{Z,\nabla_YZ} \notag \\ \nabla_Z \ang{X,Y} &= \ang{\nabla_ZX,Y} + \ang{X,\nabla_ZY}. \notag \end{align}

In view of the torsion-free condition, by adding the first two equations and subtracting the third, we get

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X\ang{Y,Z} + \nabla_Y\ang{Z,X} - \nabla_Z\ang{X,Y} = \ang{[X,Z],Y} +\ang{[Y,Z],X} + \ang{[X,Y],Z} + 2\ang{ Z,\nabla_YX}. \end{equation}

From this equation follows the important observation that the metric completely determines the connection $\nabla$:

\begin{equation}\label{det-eqn} \ang{ Z,\nabla_YX} = \frac{1}{2} \left\{ \nabla_X\ang{Y,Z} + \nabla_Y\ang{Z,X} - \nabla_Z\ang{X,Y} - \ang{[X,Z],Y} -\ang{[Y,Z],X} - \ang{[X,Y],Z} \right\}. \end{equation}

By restricting this equation over an open parametrizable subset $U$, and by choosing for $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ the coordinate local sections $\bd_i$, $\bd_j$, and $\bd_l$, we get:

\begin{equation}\notag \Gamma_{ij}^k g_{lk} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \bd_i g_{jl} + \bd_jg_{li} -\bd_l g_{ij} \right). \end{equation}

But the matrix $[g_{lk}]_{lk}$ is invertible, with inverse $[g^{lk}]_{lk}$, and hence we get

\begin{equation}\notag \Gamma_{ij}^k = \frac{1}{2} \left( \bd_i g_{jl} + \bd_jg_{li} -\bd_l g_{ij} \right)g^{lk}. \end{equation}

Let us summarize the situation, in our version of a very important result:

The Fundamental Theorem of Riemannian Geometry. Let $M$ be a riemannian manifold with metric $\ang{-,-}$. Then there is a unique $\bbr$-linear function

\begin{equation}\label{abstract-connection-eqn} \nabla : \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto \nabla_X Y, \end{equation}

that satisfies the following properties:

  1. The function $\nabla$ is $C^\infty(M)$-linear in the first argument $X$.
  2. The function $\nabla$ is a “derivation” in the second argument $Y$, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(fY) = X(f) Y + f \nabla_XY \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$ and $f\in C^\infty(M)$.
  3. The function $\nabla$ is compatible with the metric in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag X(\ang{Y,Z}) = \ang{\nabla_XY,Z} + \ang{Y,\nabla_XZ} \end{equation} for all $X,Y,Z\in \calx(M)$.
  4. The function $\nabla$ is torsion free, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_YX - \nabla_XY = [X,Y] \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$.

In particular, the metric $\ang{-,-}$ uniquely determines the vector field $\nabla_XY$ via the equation \eqref{det-eqn}, and conversely, the vector field $\nabla_XY$ may be defined by this equation. Finally, the Christoffel symbols of the connection and the metric coefficients over an open parametrizable subset are related via the equation

\begin{equation}\label{chris-metric-eqn} \Gamma_{ij}^k = \frac{1}{2} \left( \bd_i g_{jl} + \bd_jg_{li} -\bd_l g_{ij} \right)g^{lk}, \quad \forall i,j,k. \end{equation}

A function \eqref{abstract-connection-eqn} on any manifold that satisfies the first and second properties in the theorem is called an (abstract) affine or Koszul connection. The unique affine connection on a riemannian manifold that is both compatible with the metric and torsion free is called the Levi-Civita connection of the manifold. Thus, while the Fundamental Theorem guarantees that such a Levi-Civita connection exists for all riemannian manifolds via the somewhat opaque equation \eqref{det-eqn}, in the specific case of an embedded riemannian manifold where the metric is inherited from the ambient euclidean space, we have given a much more workable and concrete desciption of the connection.

Using the local formulas \eqref{chris-metric-eqn}, let’s compute the Christoffel symbols of the unit sphere $S^2\subset \bbr^3$, with respect to the spherical local parametrization map

\begin{equation}\notag (\theta,\varphi) \mapsto (\cos{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\sin{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\cos{\varphi}). \end{equation}

Here, take care to notice that $\theta$ is the azimuthal angle, while $\varphi$ is the polar angle (these are often reversed in physics and engineering!). Then, on any open subset of $S^2$ where $\theta$ and $\varphi$ serve as local coordinates, we compute the coordinate local sections

\begin{equation}\notag \bd_\theta = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin{\theta}\sin{\varphi} \\ \cos{\theta}\sin{\varphi} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad \bd_\varphi = \begin{bmatrix} \cos{\theta}\cos{\varphi} \\ \sin{\theta}\cos{\varphi} \\ -\sin{\varphi} \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

Here, we are following the common practice of writing $\bd_\theta$ and $\bd_\varphi$ in place of $\bd_1$ and $\bd_2$, respectively. The metric coefficients (and the coefficients of its inverse) are given by

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{bmatrix} g_{\theta\theta} & g_{\theta\varphi} \\ g_{\varphi\theta} & g_{\varphi\varphi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin^2{\varphi} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad \begin{bmatrix} g^{\theta\theta} & g^{\theta\varphi} \\ g^{\varphi\theta} & g^{\varphi\varphi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \csc^2{\varphi} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

The only nonzero partial derivative of the metric is

\begin{equation}\notag \bd_\varphi g_{\theta\theta} = 2\sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi}, \end{equation}

from which one concludes that there are only three (possibly) nonzero Christoffel symbols:

\begin{equation}\notag \Gamma^\theta_{\varphi\theta} = \Gamma^\theta_{\theta\varphi} = \cot{\varphi} \quad \text{and} \quad \Gamma^\varphi_{\theta\theta} = -\sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi}. \end{equation}

Parallel transport and geodesics

Let’s backtrack a bit, and explain why connections are called connections. What are they supposed to be connecting?

The answer lies in the notion of parallel transport, which in turn relies on a second type of covariant differentiation. To define this concept, we first require the notions of vector fields and functions along a curve. Throughout this section, the letter $I$ will stand for an open interval of the real line $\bbr$, that contains $0$.

Definition. Let $\gamma:I \to M$ be a smooth curve in a manifold $M$.

  1. A (smooth) vector field along $\gamma$ is a “lift” of $\gamma$ to the tangent bundle, i.e., a smooth map \begin{equation}\notag X: I \to T(M) \end{equation} such that the diagram \begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ & \color{white} T(M) \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white} \pi} \\ \color{white} I \ar@[white][ur]^{\color{white} X} \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\gamma} & \color{white} M} \end{xy} \end{equation} commutes, where $\pi$ is the natural projection. We will write $\calx(\gamma)$ for the set of all smooth vector fields along $\gamma$.
  2. A (smooth) function along $\gamma$ is simply a smooth function in $C^\infty(I)$.

There is a (perhaps) disappointing dissimilarity between the definitions of smooth vector fields and smooth functions along a curve $\gamma$. However, they can be brought together under the same scheme of “lifts” if we instead define a smooth function along $\gamma$ to be a lift of $\gamma$ to the trivial (basic) vector bundle $M \times \bbr$. Precisely, we would define such a function to be a smooth map

\begin{equation}\notag \widehat{f}: I \to M \times \bbr \end{equation}
