that “lifts” the curve $\gamma$ to the trivial bundle, in the sense that the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ & \color{white} M \times \bbr \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white} \pi_1} \\ \color{white} I \ar@[white][ur]^{\color{white} \widehat{f}} \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\gamma} & \color{white} M} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes, where $\pi_1: M \times \bbr \to M$ is the natural projection onto the first cartesian factor. This means that $\widehat{f}$ is given by a formula of the form

\begin{equation}\label{chair-eqn} \widehat{f}(t) = (\gamma(t),f(t)), \end{equation}

where $f$ is a smooth function in $C^\infty(I)$. We conceptualize the formula \eqref{chair-eqn} as the scalar $f(t)$ “attached” to the point $\gamma(t)$ on the curve, so that in this sense, a function along $\gamma$ is viewed as a collection of scalars (i.e., a scalar field) along the curve. But the lift is obviously completely determined by the function $f\in C^\infty(I)$, which means that we may as well define smooth functions along $\gamma$ as functions in $C^\infty(I)$, which is what we did above.

Note that if $M$ is embedded in $\bbr^s$, then a vector field along $\gamma$ may be uniquely specified by instead giving a smooth map

\begin{equation}\notag X: I \to \bbr^s \end{equation}

such that $X_t \in T_{\gamma(t)}(M)$ for all $t\in I$.

Exercise. Let $\gamma:I \to M$ be a smooth curve in a manifold $M$. Prove that the set $\calx(\gamma)$ is a $C^\infty(I)$-module in a natural way.

Given a smooth function $f\in C^\infty(I)$ along the curve $\gamma$, we already have a notion of the derivative of $f$. Indeed, such a derivative is simply the smooth function

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{Df}{dt} \defeq f’, \end{equation}

where $f’\in C^\infty(I)$ is defined by

\begin{equation}\notag f’(t) = f_{\ast,t}(1) \in \bbr. \end{equation}

However, just as with Levi-Civita connections, our goal is to extend the operator $D/dt$ to act on vector fields along $\gamma$, producing a vector field $DX/dt$.

Definition. Let $\gamma$ be a smooth curve in an embedded riemannian manifold $M$. The covariant derivative along $\gamma$ is the $\bbr$-linear map $D/dt$ which acts on both $\calx(\gamma)$ and $C^\infty(I)$ as follows:

  1. Its action on vector fields along $\gamma$ yields a function \begin{equation}\notag D/dt: \calx(\gamma) \to \calx(\gamma), \quad X\mapsto DX/dt \end{equation} given by \begin{equation}\notag (DX/dt)_t = X_{\ast,t}(1)|_{T_{\gamma(t)}(M)}, \end{equation} for $t\in I$.
  2. Its action on functions along $\gamma$ yields a function \begin{equation}\notag D/dt: C^\infty(I) \to C^\infty(I), \quad f\mapsto Df/dt \end{equation} given by \begin{equation}\notag Df/dt= f’, \end{equation} for $f\in C^\infty(I)$.

As with the Levi-Civita connection, it is not obvious that $DX/dt$ is actually a smooth vector field. However, we will take this for granted in this post.

I suggest that you give a go at the following exercise, which lists some fundamental properties of the covariant derivative along curves.

Exercise. Let $\gamma$ be a smooth curve in an embedded riemannian manifold $M$ with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$.

  1. Prove that $D/dt$ actually is $\bbr$-linear, for both its action on vector fields and functions along $\gamma$.
  2. Prove that $D/dt$ is a “derivation” with respect to the $C^\infty(I)$-module structures, i.e., prove that \begin{equation}\notag D(fX)/dt= (Df/dt) X + f DX/dt. \end{equation} and \begin{equation}\notag D(fg)/dt= (Df/dt) g + fDg/dt \end{equation} for all $X\in \calx(\gamma)$ and $f,g\in C^\infty(I)$.
  3. Suppose that a vector field $X\in \calx(\gamma)$ is induced by a global vector field $Y\in \calx(M)$, in the sense that $X_t = Y_{\gamma(t)}$ for all $t\in I$. Prove that \begin{equation}\notag (DX/dt)_t = \nabla_{\gamma’(t)} Y \end{equation} for all $t\in I$, where $\gamma’(t) = \gamma_{\ast,t}(1)$.

In the special case that $M=\bbr^n$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag DX/dt = X’, \end{equation}

where $X’(t) = X_{\ast,t}(1)$. It follows that if $DX/dt=0$, then the vectors $X_t$ are parallel to each other, as $t$ varies over $I$. In the case of a general manifold, we take the vanishing condition $DX/dt=0$ as the definition of parallel:

Definition. Let $\gamma:I \to M$ be a smooth curve in an embedded Riemannian manifold $M$. We shall say a vector field $X$ along $\gamma$ is parallel if $DX/dt=0$.

There is a particularly popular example of a parallel vector field on the sphere $S^2$ that virtually every introductory reference on differential geometry covers. This post will be no exception. But first, let’s start out with a related parallel vector field on the unit circle $S^1$ in $\bbr^2$.

Let $\gamma:\bbr \to S^1$ be the smooth path given by

\begin{equation}\notag \gamma(t) = (\sin{t},\cos{t}). \end{equation}

Define the vector field $X:\bbr \to \bbr^2$ along $\gamma$ by

\begin{equation}\notag X_t = (\cos{t},-\sin{t}). \end{equation}

One then easily computes $\nabla_\gamma X=0$, and hence $X$ is a parallel vector field on $S^1$.

Now, if $\gamma:[0,\pi/2] \to \bbr^3$ is a similar path

Spectra

Manifolds appear in physical theory as geometric objects whose points correspond to states of the (classical) systems under consideration. For example, the states of a classical hamiltonian system are encoded in a manifold called the momentum phase space, which is an even-dimensional manifold with local coordinates that are divided in half: the first half describes the static (generalized) positions of the constituent objects of the system, while the second half describes the dynamic (generalized) momenta of those objects. The dynamical states of the system correspond to points of the phase space manifold.

In this paradigm, an observable is any smooth real-valued function of position and momentum. Thus, if the phase space is $M$, then the set of observables is precisely the commutative $\bbr$-algebra $C^\infty(M)$. The following ontological principle holds that the algebra of observables completely determines the system:

The Observability Principle. What exists is only that which can be observed.

In other words, there should be no more information encoded in the phase space $M$ of a dynamical system that isn’t recoverable from the algebra $C^\infty(M)$. In the other direction, since $C^\infty(M)$ is uniquely obtainable from $M$, neither should there be more information in $C^\infty(M)$ that isn’t already in $M$. Therefore, a corollary to The Observability Principle is that $M$ and $C^\infty(M)$ are entirely equivalent objects.

The Observability Principle is certainly a bold claim, and I suspect that the philosophically minded reader might be slightly shocked to see such an assertion put forth with no supporting argument or evidence. However, regardless of what you might think of its truth in our real world, as I will explain in this section, in the purely, idealized mathematical world, it is true!

The mechanism which mathematically actualizes the principle is the theory of spectra. This theory is just one instance of the broader observation by mathematicians of a frequently occurring duality between geometry and algebra.

In fact, if you read the previous post in this series, you have already seen a hint of spectra. In that post, the main geometric category of interest was the category of pointed manifolds, which has as its objects all pairs $(M,p)$ consisting of a point $p$ on a manifold $M$. However, when we proved that the space of derivations on the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$ was isomorphic to the Zariski tangent space, we appealed to a purely algebraic result stated in the framework of the category of pointed algebras. This latter category has as its objects all pairs $(A,\dev)$, where $\dev:A \to \bbr$ is a homomorphism of $\bbr$-algebras. We observed that the two categories are linked by an evident functor given by

\begin{equation}\notag (M,p) \mapsto (C^\infty_{M,p},\dev_p), \end{equation}

where $\dev_p: C^\infty_{M,p} \to \bbr$ was the homomorphism that evaluates a germ at the point $p$. This was our first hint of the link between points of the manifold $M$ and $\bbr$-algebra homomorphisms.

Now, the theory of spectra can be described simply as this identification of points and homomorphisms carried to its logical conclusion. Nothing more, nothing less.

Definition. Let $A$ be a commutative $\bbr$-algebra. The spectrum of $A$, denoted $\Spec{A}$, is the set of all unital $\bbr$-algebra homomorphisms $A\to \bbr$.

First, I should mention that there are multiple definitions of spectra in the literature. For example, what I have written as $\Spec{A}$ is often interpreted in scheme-theoretic algebraic geometry as the prime spectrum of $A$, which is the set of all prime ideals of $A$. These latter types of spectra will make a brief appearance later.

As I mentioned above, the jumping off point for the theory of spectra is the identification of geometric points with algebra homomorphisms. Thus, this theory demands that $\Spec{A}$ should be treated as a geometric object, and $A$ should be viewed as an algebra of real-valued functions on it. But, does this actually work?

Not always.

To see the problem, let’s suppose that we write $\Fun(\Spec{A})$ for the set of all real-valued functions $\Spec{A} \to \bbr$. Notice that $\Fun(\Spec{A})$ is a commutative $\bbr$-algebra, with respect to the evident pointwise operations. There is a natural map given by evaluation, namely

\begin{equation}\label{func-eqn} \Theta: A \to \Fun(\Spec{A}), \quad \left[\Theta(f)\right](p) = p(f), \end{equation}

for all $f\in A$ and all $p\in \Spec{A}$. You may easily check that $\Theta$ is a homomorphism of $\bbr$-algebras, and it is through this map that we are to view elements $f\in A$ as real-valued functions on $\Spec{A}$. However, $\Theta$ may not always provide a faithful representation of $A$ as a ring of functions, as it may not be injective.

The algebras for which $\Theta$ is injective have a special name: they are called geometric algebras. These are algebras for which the intersection $\bigcap_{p\in \Spec{A}} \ker{p}$ is zero.

Theorem (Geometric algebras have faithful representations as functions). Let $A$ be a commutative $\bbr$-algebra and suppose $\Theta$ is the natural map defined above \eqref{func-eqn}. Then $\ker{\Theta}$ is the intersection $\bigcap_{p\in \Spec{A}} \ker{p}$.

Since every $\ker{p}$ is a maximal ideal in $A$, it is thus easy to cook up examples of non-geometric algebras. For one special class of examples, just consider algebras that have only one maximal ideal, i.e., local algebras.

The class of geometric algebras is closely related to the class of semiprimitive algebras.

Some fundamental natural isomorphisms

We are on our way to defining tensors on manifolds. However, as you may already know, a perusal of the literature shows that there seems to be as many ways to define tensors as there are textbook authors. Obviously, this makes it difficult for beginners to compare the treatments in different references. All the definitions are ultimately the “same,” but hardly any references (if any) explicitly explain why they are the “same.”

The reason “same” is enclosed in quotation marks is that the various modules of tensors that you find in the literature are not literally equal to each other, but rather are naturally isomorphic. Intuitively, to say that two modules are naturally isomorphic means that they are connected by an isomorphism that does not depend on artificial choices. The prototypical example of a natural isomorphism is the one between a finite-dimensional vector space $V$ and its double dual $(V^\ast)^\ast$. In this case, the natural isomorphism is

\begin{equation}\notag \Theta: V \to (V^\ast)^\ast, \quad v \mapsto \Theta_v, \end{equation}

where $\Theta_v(\alpha) = \alpha(v)$. This isomorphism is natural (in the technical sense) because its definition is natural (in the colloquial sense). On the other hand, the typical method for showing that $V$ is isomorphic to the dual $V^\ast$ is not natural, because it depends on an artificial choice of basis. Philosophically, naturality is particularly important because it means that constructions on fibers globalize to constructions on an entire vector bundle.

There are broadly two types of definitions of tensors on a manifold: The first are the tensor definitions, where a tensor is defined to be either a global section of a tensor product bundle, or an element in the tensor product of the modules of global sections; the second are the Hom definitions, where a tensor is defined to be either a global section of a Hom bundle, or an element in the Hom of the modules of global sections. So, in both types of definitions, either a tensor is a field, i.e., a section of a vector bundle, or else an element of a tensor product or Hom module.

Before we explore tensors, my goal in this section is to equip you with the natural isomorphisms that you’ll need to move back and forth between these various definitions of tensors. That tensors can be defined and specified in so many ways can be incredibly confusing and frustrating at first. However, as you will see, tensors are extremely flexible and powerful objects, and so in this light it’s perhaps not surprising that they can be understood from so many different angles.

Let’s begin.

If $P$ is a module over a commutative ring $A$, then there is a natural $A$-bilinear pairing

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{-,-} : P \otimes_A P^\ast \to A, \quad (v,\alpha) \mapsto \ang{v,\alpha} \defeq \alpha(v) \end{equation}

which, in the context of tensor analysis, is often called the contraction mapping. Of course, there is a similar pairing with the tensor factors permuted:

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{-,-} : P^\ast \otimes_A P \to A, \quad (\alpha,v) \mapsto \ang{\alpha,v} \defeq \alpha(v). \end{equation}

If $P$ is a free $A$-module of finite rank, say with basis $e_1,\ldots,e_n$, then we may define functionals $\eta^1,\ldots,\eta^n\in P^\ast$ by setting

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{\eta^i,e_j} = \delta^i_j \defeq \begin{cases} 1 & : i=j, \\ 0 & : i\neq j, \end{cases} \end{equation}

and extending by linearity. In this case, we refer to $e_1,\ldots,e_n$ and $\eta^1,\ldots,\eta^n$ as dually paired bases of $P$ and its $A$-linear dual $P^\ast$.

These contraction mappings may be used to define several fundamental homomorphisms involving a single tensor product:

Theorem on Contraction Homomorphisms, Module Version. Let $P_1$ and $P_2$ be modules over a commutative ring $A$.

  1. There is a natural homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Delta: P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast \to \Hom_A{(P_1^\ast \otimes_A P_2, A)} \end{equation} of $A$-modules given on pure tensors by \begin{equation}\notag \left[ \Delta(v_1 \otimes \alpha^2)\right](\alpha^1 \otimes v_2) = \ang{\alpha^1,v_1}\ang{\alpha^2,v_2}. \end{equation}
  2. There is a natural homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Theta: P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast \to \Hom_A{(P_2, P_1)} \end{equation} of $A$-modules given on pure tensors by \begin{equation}\notag \left[\Theta(v_1 \otimes \alpha^2)\right](v_2) = \ang{\alpha^2,v_2}v_1. \end{equation}
  3. There is a natural homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Lambda: P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast \to \Hom_A{(P_1^\ast,P_2^\ast)} \end{equation} of $A$-modules given on pure tensors by \begin{equation}\notag \left[\Lambda(v_1\otimes \alpha^2)\right](\alpha^1) = \ang{\alpha^1,v_1} \alpha^2. \end{equation}

If both $P_1$ and $P_2$ are finitely generated and projective (in particular, if they are finite-dimensional vector spaces over a field), then $\Delta$, $\Theta$, and $\Lambda$ are isomorphisms.

I would suggest that you try to prove this theorem on your own. If you’re not comfortable with projective modules, you may assume that all modules are finite-dimensional vector spaces over a field.

Though these maps are all defined without mention of bases (they are natural!), it is still instructive to write them out in terms of components. To do so, we will assume that $P_1$ and $P_2$ are both equal to a module $P$ which is finitely-generated and free with dually paired bases $e_1,\ldots,e_n$ and $\eta^1,\ldots,\eta^n$. Then, we suppose that we have the following objects, each resolved in terms of the associated bases, with summation over repeated indices implied:

  1. a tensor $T = T^i_j \ e_i \otimes \eta^j \in P \otimes_A P^\ast$,
  2. a second tensor $S = S^k_l \ \eta^l \otimes e_k \in P^\ast \otimes_A P$,
  3. a vector $v = v^k e_k \in P$, and
  4. a covector $f = f_k \eta^k \in P^\ast$.

Then, I will leave it to you to verify that:

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\Delta(T)\right](S) = T^i_j S^k_l \ang{\eta^l, e_i}\ang{\eta^j,e_k} = T^i_j S_i^j, \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\Theta(T)\right](v) = T^i_j v^k \ang{\eta^j,e_k}e_i = T^i_j v^j e_i, \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\Lambda(T)\right](f) = T^i_j f_k \ang{\eta^k,e_i}\eta^j = T^i_j f_i \eta^j. \end{equation}

Notice that the effect of these homomorphisms is to set pairs of upper and lower indices in the components equal, and then sum over them. This is the general flavor of these types of maps.

There are many similar homomorphisms that involve more than two modules. For example, there is a natural homomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \Xi: P_1^\ast \otimes_A P_2 \otimes_A P_3^\ast \to \Hom_A(P_1, P_2 \otimes_A P_3^\ast) \end{equation}

given on pure tensors by

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\Xi(f^1 \otimes v_2 \otimes f^3)\right](v_1) = \ang{f^1,v_1} \ v_2 \otimes f^3. \end{equation}

But there is also a natural homomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \Pi: P_1^\ast \otimes_A P_2 \otimes_A P_3^\ast \to \Hom_A(P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast, P_3^\ast) \end{equation}

given on pure tensors by

\begin{equation}\notag \left[\Pi(f^1 \otimes v_2 \otimes f^3)\right](v_1 \otimes f^2) = \ang{f^1,v_1}\ang{f^2,v_2} f^3. \end{equation}

Both $\Xi$ and $\Pi$ are isomorphisms if the modules involved are finitely generated and projective.

I will leave you to imagine even more versions of these homomorphisms. It would be extraordinarily cumbersome (and ultimately useless) for me to attempt to list them all, so you need to see a few examples of how they work, and then you can write down your own. By now, after having seen five examples, this should be no problem. The common feature they all share is that they identify (via contraction mappings) a Hom module of linear maps with certain tensor products. Generically, we will refer to these types of homomorphisms as contraction-type homomorphisms, or contraction-type isomorphisms if all modules in sight are finitely generated and projective.

One final observation before we move onto the bundle versions of these maps: Notice that the domains of the maps $\Xi$ and $\Pi$ above coincide, and hence their codomains are naturally isomorphic (in the case that the modules are finitely generated and projective). But the difference between the codomains is that the module $P_2$ swapped slots in the Hom, while at the same time being dualized. This is an example of a general and useful heuristic:

Suppose that all modules under consideration are finitely generated and projective.

  1. A module can swap slots in a Hom, while either adding a duality (if there wasn’t one already) or removing it.
  2. If all modules are swapped out of a slot in a Hom, then the ring $A$ is written in the empty slot.

For another example of the heuristic in (1.), note that there is a natural isomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \Hom_A(P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast\otimes_A P_3, P_4^\ast \otimes_A P_5 ) \cong \Hom_A(P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast \otimes_A P_4, P_3^\ast \otimes_A P_5 ). \end{equation}

Notice that the $P_3$ and $P_4$ modules swapped slots, with $P_3$ gaining a duality and $P_4$ losing one. To prove that these Hom-modules are isomorphic, we note that they are both naturally isomorphic to the tensor product

\begin{equation}\label{blinds-eqn} P_1^\ast \otimes_A P_2 \otimes_A P_3^\ast \otimes_A P_4^\ast \otimes_A P_5 \end{equation}

via a pair of contraction-type isomorphisms. Can you describe them? If we were to swap both $P_4$ and $P_5$ from the second slot of the first Hom, the heuristic in (2.) says that there is a natural isomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \Hom_A(P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast\otimes_A P_3, P_4^\ast \otimes_A P_5 )\cong \Hom_A(P_1 \otimes_A P_2^\ast\otimes_A P_3 \otimes_A P_4 \otimes_A P_5^\ast, A ). \end{equation}

Again, this isomorphism results because both of these Hom-modules are naturally isomorphic to the tensor product \eqref{blinds-eqn}.

Now, I mentioned at the beginning of this section that one important consequence of naturality is that such isomorphisms may be “bundled” across fibers to yield corresponding isomorphisms of vector bundles. With slightly more precision, I state:

Theorem on Contraction Isomorphisms, Bundle Version. There are isomorphisms of vector bundles whose restrictions to fibers are contraction-type isomorphisms. For example, if $E_1$ and $E_2$ are vector bundles over a manifold $M$, there is an isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag E_1 \otimes_M E_2^\ast \xrightarrow{\cong} \Hom_M(E_2, E_1) \end{equation} of vector bundles whose restrictions to fibers coincide with the isomorphism $\Theta$ from the module-level theorem. Like their module-level cousins, these vector bundle isomorphisms are generically called contraction-type isomorphisms.

It’s not only the contraction-type isomorphisms that will prove crucial to understanding tensors on manifolds. We also need the isomorphisms in:

Fundamental Lemma on $\Gamma$, Hom and $\otimes$. Let $E$ and $F$ be vector bundles over a manifold $M$.

  1. There is a natural isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Psi: \Gamma\left(\Hom_M{(E,F)}\right) \xrightarrow{\cong} \Hom_{C^\infty(M)}\left(\Gamma(E),\Gamma(F) \right) \end{equation} of $C^\infty(M)$-modules given by \begin{equation}\notag \left[\left(\Psi(\sigma)\right)(\tau)\right]_p = \sigma_p(\tau_p). \end{equation}
  2. There is a natural isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Sigma: \Gamma(E) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \Gamma(F) \xrightarrow{\cong} \Gamma(E\otimes_M F) \end{equation} of $C^\infty(M)$-modules given on pure tensors by \begin{equation}\notag \left[\Sigma \left(\sigma \otimes \tau\right)\right]_p = \sigma_p \otimes \tau_p. \end{equation}

By specializing $F$ to the trival bundle $M \times \bbr$ in part (1.) of the Fundamental Lemma, we get:

Corollary on Global Sections of Duals. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$. There is a natural isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \Psi: \Gamma\left(E^\ast\right) \xrightarrow{\cong} \Gamma(E)^\ast \end{equation} of $C^\infty(M)$-modules given by \begin{equation}\notag \left[\left(\Psi(\sigma)\right)(f)\right]_p = \sigma_p(f(p)), \end{equation} for $\sigma \in \Gamma(E^\ast)$, $f\in C^\infty(M)$, and $p\in M$.

Tensor fields

Blah!

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold with tangent bundle $T(M)$ and cotangent bundle $T^\ast(M)$. Given integers $r,s\geq 0$, a tensor of type $\binom{r}{s}$ is a global section of the vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \underbrace{T(M) \otimes_M \cdots \otimes_M T(M)}_{\text{$r$ factors}} \otimes_M \underbrace{T^\ast(M) \otimes_M \cdots \otimes_M T^\ast(M)}_{\text{$s$ factors}}. \end{equation}

If both $r=0$ and $s=0$, then a tensor of type $\binom{0}{0}$ is a global section of the trivial vector bundle $M \times \bbr$, i.e., a smooth function on $M$.

Blah!

Theorem on Alternate Definitions of Tensors. Let $M$ be a manifold with tangent bundle $T(M)$ and cotangent bundle $T^\ast(M)$, and let $r,s,\geq 0$ be integers. A unique tensor of type $\binom{r}{s}$ can be (naturally!) specified by giving one of the following objects:

  1. A global section of the vector bundle \begin{equation}\notag T(M)^{\otimes r} \otimes_M T^\ast(M)^{\otimes s}. \end{equation}
  2. A global section of the vector bundle \begin{equation}\notag \Hom_M\left( T^\ast(M)^{\otimes r} \otimes_M T(M)^{\otimes s}, M \times \bbr\right). \end{equation}
  3. A linear map in \begin{equation}\notag \Hom_{C^\infty(M)}\left( \calx^\ast(M)^{\otimes r} \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M)^{\otimes s}, C^\infty(M)\right). \end{equation}
  4. A tensor in \begin{equation}\notag \calx(M)^{\otimes r} \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx^\ast(M)^{\otimes s}. \end{equation}

For practice, I suggest that you use the natural isomorphisms above to link these various descriptions of tensors together. For example, let’s suppose that we consider $\binom{1}{1}$-tensors, which are in the module

\begin{equation}\label{table-eqn} \Gamma(T(M) \otimes_M T^\ast(M)) \end{equation}

of global sections. But by the Fundamental Lemma, this module is naturally isomorphic to the module

\begin{equation}\notag \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx^\ast(M) \end{equation}

which appears in part (4.) of the theorem. On the other hand, by applying the global section functor $\Gamma$ to an isomorphism in the Theorem on Natural Isomorphisms (bundle version), we see that \eqref{table-eqn} is also naturally isomorphic to the module

\begin{equation}\notag \Gamma\left( \Hom_M \left(T^\ast(M) \otimes_M T(M),M \times \bbr \right) \right) \end{equation}

which appears in part (2.). What isomorphism would you use to show that \eqref{table-eqn} is isomorphic to the module

\begin{equation}\notag \Hom_{C^\infty(M)}\left( \calx^\ast(M)\otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M), C^\infty(M)\right) \end{equation}

in part (3.)?

To obtain the equivalent descriptions in the theorem for tensors of type other than $\binom{1}{1}$, you’ll need to use the “variations” on the natural isomorphisms like \eqref{iterated2-eqn}. Again, I suggest that you explore various cases with small values of $r$ and $s$, just to practice with the isomorphisms.

Now, I should also mention that the list of four equivalent descriptions of tensors given in the theorem is not exhaustive. For example, you will often see the so-called Riemann curvature tensor defined as a $C^\infty(M)$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag R: \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \end{equation}

where it is claimed that $R$ is a $\binom{1}{3}$-tensor. But this description of $R$ doesn’t fit with any of our four descriptions of tensors! However, can you see how to use the isomorphism \eqref{book-eqn} to confirm that $R$ can, indeed, be identified with a $\binom{1}{3}$-tensor?

Thus, you can see that there are very many ways to specify tensors. It would be quite difficult (and useless) for me to try to enumerate all these different ways; rather, it would be much more beneficial for you to spend time on your own getting used to the mechanics of these natural isomorphisms, so that you’ll know how to recognize tensors when you see them.

Locally trivial vector bundles

We defined in an earlier post a set-theoretic vector bundle to be a surjective function

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: E\to B \end{equation}

of sets such that each fiber

\begin{equation}\notag E_b \defeq \pi^{-1}(b) \end{equation}

is an $\bbr$-vector space. If

\begin{equation}\notag \pi’:E’ \to B \end{equation}

is a second set-theoretic vector bundle, then a morphism of set-theoretic vector bundles from $\pi$ to $\pi’$ is a function $\theta: E\to E’$ such that the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}E \ar@[white][rr]^{\color{white}\theta} \ar@[white][dr]_{\color{white}\pi} & & \color{white} E’ \ar@[white][dl]^{\color{white}\pi’} \\ & \color{white}B} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes. We say $\theta$ is an isomorphism if it is bijective, in which case its inverse $\theta^{-1}:E’ \to E$ is easily seen to be a morphism of set-theoretic vector bundles.

Now, suppose that we are given a set-theoretic vector bundle $\pi:E \to B$. From the fibers of $\pi$ we may form the disjoint union

\begin{equation}\notag E’ = \bigsqcup_{b\in B} E_b, \end{equation}

and then let $\pi’: E’ \to B$ be the natural projection. Then there is an evident isomorphism of set-theoretic vector bundles $\theta: E \to E’$ which is given on fibers by

\begin{equation}\notag \theta_b: E_b \to E’_b = \{b\} \times E_b, \quad e \mapsto (b,e). \end{equation}

This shows that an equivalent way to conceptualize a set-theoretic vector bundle (up to isomorphism) is as an indexed (or parametrized) collection $\{E_b\}_{b\in B}$ of vector spaces.

In this post, we shall consider set-theoretic vector bundles in the category of smooth manifolds:

Definition. A smooth vector bundle is a set-theoretic vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ where the sets $E$ and $M$ are both smooth manifolds, and with respect to these structures, $\pi$ is a smooth map. The spaces $E$ and $M$ are called the total and base spaces of the vector bundle.

A morphism of smooth vector bundles, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag \theta: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi’:E’\to M), \end{equation}

is a function $\theta:E\to E’$ which is both a morphism of the underlying set-theoretic vector bundles and is smooth. (Note that this a slight abuse of notation, by using the symbol $\theta$ twice.)

The identity morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \id_\pi: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi:E\to M) \end{equation}

of the smooth vector bundle $\pi$ is defined to be the morphism $\id_\pi = \id_E : E\to E$.

If

\begin{equation}\notag \theta’: (\pi:E’\to M) \to (\pi'':E''\to M) \end{equation}

is a second morphism of smooth vector bundles, the composition

\begin{equation}\notag \theta’ \circ \theta: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi'':E''\to M) \end{equation}

is defined to be the standard composition $\theta’ \circ \theta:E\to E’’$ of functions.

With these objects, morphisms, identity morphisms, and composition law, we obtain the category of smooth vector bundles over $M$, denoted $\Vect_M$.

As with set-theoretic vector bundles, we may conceptualize a smooth vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ as an indexed collection $\{E_p\}_{p\in M}$ of vector spaces. This time, however, the indexing set is a smooth manifold, and thus we may conceptualize the collection $\{E_p\}_{p\in M}$ as a “smoothly parametrized” family of vector spaces. Here, “smoothness” of the parametrization is encoded in the smoothness of $E$, $M$ and $\pi$.

For example, we might consider the collection $\{T_p(M)\}_{p\in M}$ of all tangent spaces over a smooth manifold $M$. Intuitively, the vector spaces in this collection should “vary smoothly” with $p$. But as we saw in a previous post, the tangent bundle

\begin{equation}\notag T(M) \defeq \bigsqcup_{p\in M} T_p(M) \end{equation}

has a smooth manifold structure with respect to which the natural projection $\pi:T(M) \to M$ is smooth. The fiber

\begin{equation}\notag \pi^{-1}(p) = \{p\} \times T_p(M) \end{equation}

may be naturally identified with $T_p(M)$, and thus the family of vector spaces $\{T_p(M)\}_{p\in M}$ may be naturally identified with the smooth vector bundle $\pi:T(M) \to M$. This identification makes precise our intuition that the tangent space $T_p(M)$ should “vary smoothly” with $p$.

We may manufacture a smooth vector bundle over a smooth manifold $M$ by taking the natural projection

\begin{equation}\notag \pi_1: M \times \bbr^k \to M \end{equation}

onto the first cartesian factor. If $\pi:E \to M$ is a smooth vector bundle which is isomorphic to $\pi_1$ (in the category $\Vect_M$), then $\pi$ is called a trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$.

Not all smooth vector bundles are trivial; in fact, we saw in our earlier post on tangent bundles that the tangent bundle of the sphere $S^2$ is not trivial. However, if $M$ is any manifold, we also saw that there is an open covering $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ of $M$ for which the restricted smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: \pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i \end{equation}

is trivial, as a smooth vector bundle over the open set $U_i$. Thus, while the tangent bundle $T(M)$ may not be globally trivial, it is always locally trivial in this sense.

The category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles will be one of the main focuses of this post. Let’s formally define this category:

Definition. A locally trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$ is a smooth vector bundle $\pi:E\to M$ for which there exists an open cover $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ such that each restricted smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: \pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i \end{equation}

is isomorphic (in the category $\Vect_{U_i}$) to the trivial smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi_1: U_i \times \bbr^k \to U_i. \end{equation}

In this case, the open cover $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ is called a trivializing open cover of the vector bundle, the vector bundle is said to trivialize over $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$, and isomorphisms

\begin{equation}\notag \Phi_i: (U_i \times \bbr^k \to U_i) \to (\pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i) \end{equation}

of smooth vector bundles over $U_i$ are called (local) trivializations.

The category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles is the full subcategory of $\Vect_M$ whose objects are the locally trivial smooth vector bundles of rank $k$, for all $k$.

In many texts, locally trivial smooth vector bundles are the only types of vector bundles considered, and hence these objects are usually just called vector bundles. We shall follow the same practice:

Terminology and Notation. For the rest of this post, a vector bundle will be a locally trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$, for some $k$. A morphism of vector bundles will be a morphism of smooth vector bundles. In particular, the notation $\Vect_M$ will be re-defined to stand for the category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles over $M$.

Furthermore, when no confusion is likely to arise, we will reference a vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ by its total space $E$ only. Accordingly, a morphism of vector bundles

\begin{equation}\notag \theta: (\pi:E \to M) \to (\pi’: E’\to M) \end{equation}

will be denoted only by the map $\theta:E \to E’$ on total spaces.

One of the most useful properties of morphisms of (locally trivial smooth!) vector bundles is that the isomorphism property may be tested locally on the fibers. Precisely:

Fiberwise Criterion for Vector Bundle Isomorphisms. Let $\theta: E \to E’$ be a morphism of vector bundles over a common base space $M$. Then $\theta$ is an isomorphism of vector bundles if and only if each induced map $\alpha_p:E_p \to E’_p$ on the fibers is a linear isomorphism.

I suggest that you attempt to prove this theorem on your own. You will see that the local triviality of the vector bundles is used in a crucial way.

Sections of vector bundles

In this section we study smooth sections of (locally trivial) vector bundles. We have already seen examples of these gadgets in two places: (1) One guise of smooth vector fields on a manifold is as smooth (global) sections of the tangent bundle, and (2) smooth sections of the trivial vector bundle $M \times \bbr \to M$ are exactly smooth functions on $M$. Indeed, we covered (1) in this post, while to verify that claim (2) is true, we note that a smooth map $\sigma: M \to M \times \bbr$ of the form

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma(p) = (p,f(p)) \end{equation}

is uniquely determined by the smooth function $f\in C^\infty(M)$. In general, the definition is as follows:

Definition. Let $\pi:E \to M$ be a vector bundle and $U\subseteq M$ a nonempty open set. A local section of the vector bundle over $U$ is a function $\sigma:U\to E$ such that $\pi\circ \sigma = \id_U$. We shall say $\sigma$ is a smooth local section if it is a smooth map between manifolds.

We write $\Gamma(U,E)$ for the set of smooth local sections over $U$. If the vector bundle $E$ is clear from context, we will write $\Gamma(U)$ in place of $\Gamma(U,E)$. Sections in $\Gamma(M)$ are called smooth global sections.

We will often write the functional value $\sigma(p) \in E_p$ of a section as $\sigma_p$. If the section happens to have an index, like $\sigma_i$, then we will write $\sigma_i|_p$ in place of $(\sigma_i)_p$.

Exercise. Given two smooth local sections $\sigma,\tau\in \Gamma(U)$, we define their sum, denoted $\sigma+\tau$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (\sigma+\tau)_p = \sigma_p + \tau_p, \end{equation}

where the sum on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. If $f\in C^\infty(U)$ is a function, we define the product of $f$ and $\sigma$, denoted $f\sigma$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (f\sigma)_p = f(p) \sigma_p, \end{equation}

where the product on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. Prove that with respect to these operations, the set $\Gamma(U)$ is a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

A smoothly varying collection of bases of the fibers of a vector bundle is called a smooth local frame, which is terminology that you should recognize from the earlier post on vector fields. Officially:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Then a collection of smooth local sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ is called a smooth local frame over $U$ if

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1|_p,\ldots,\vartheta_k|_p \in E_p \end{equation}

is a basis for all $p\in U$.

One of the main uses of smooth local frames is to test for smoothness of general sections:

Theorem (Componentwise criterion for smoothness). Let $\pi:E\to M$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Given a function $\sigma:U\to E$ (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous) such that $\pi \circ \sigma = \id_U$ and a smooth local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k\in \Gamma(U)$, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p = \sigma^i(p) \vartheta_i|_p \end{equation}

for each $p\in U$, where $\sigma^i: U \to \bbr$ are uniquely determined real-valued functions (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous). These functions are called the component functions of $\sigma$ with respect to the local frame.

Then $\sigma$ is smooth if and only if each component function $\sigma^i$ is smooth.

Exercise. Prove that the local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ forms a basis as a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

Given a manifold $M$, we recall that we proved the assignments $U \mapsto C^\infty(U)$ and $U \mapsto \calx(U)$ are examples of sheaves in this post. Had we known at the time that $\calx(U)$ could be viewed as smooth local sections of a certain vector bundle, then we could have applied the following general result:

Theorem (Sections of vector bundles form sheaves). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$. If for each open set $U\subseteq M$ we define $\cale(U) = \Gamma(U,E)$, then $\cale$ is a $C^\infty_M$-module, where $C^\infty_M$ is the sheaf of smooth functions on $M$. The sheaf $\cale$ is called the sheaf of sections of the vector bundle $E$.

We learned the general definition of a sheaf in the previous post. When we say that $\cale$ is a sheaf of $C^\infty_M$-modules, we really mean that $\cale$ is a sheaf of modules over the sheaf of rings $C^\infty_M$. For the general definition of these latter types of objects, see here.

You also learned the definition of stalks of structure sheaves of ringed spaces in the previous post. It turns out that such stalks may be defined for any sheaf and, in particular, they may be defined for sheaves of sections of vector bundles. You should have no trouble, therefore, in adapting the definition of stalks in the previous post to apply to our sheaves of sections. Otherwise, see here for the official definition.

Let’s set some notation for stalks:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. We will write $\cale_p$ for the stalk of $\cale$ at $p\in M$. Elements in $\cale_p$ will be called germs of sections.

Exercise.

  1. Prove that each stalk $\cale_p$ is a module over the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$ with respect the evident operations. (By now, I’m hoping that this proof is routine!)
  2. For each $p\in M$, recall that there is a well-defined homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \dev_p:C^\infty_{M,p} \to \bbr, \quad f \mapsto f(p), \end{equation} of $\bbr$-algebras. By restricting scalars along this map, a fiber $E_p$ of the vector bundle may be given a $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module structure. With respect to this structure, prove that the map \begin{equation}\notag e_p: \cale_p \to E_p, \quad \sigma \mapsto \sigma_p, \end{equation} is a well-defined homomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules.

Remember that the germs in the stalk $\cale_p$ are actually equivalence classes of sections $\sigma$ defined on open neighborhoods of $p$, so my definition “$\sigma \mapsto \sigma_p$” of the evaluation map $e_p$ is technically an abuse of notation. However, as I’ve mentioned in the past, this is a typical abuse, and going forth I will use it without further comment.

Take care to notice the difference between $\cale_p$ and $E_p$ for a vector bundle $E$ over a manifold $M$. The former is the stalk of the sheaf of sections at the point $p\in M$, while the latter is the fiber over $p$. While these are different objects, there is, nevertheless, a tight link between them:

Theorem. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. For each $p\in M$, let $\mfm_p$ denote the maximal ideal of the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$. The sequence

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \to \mfm_p \to \cale_p \xrightarrow{e_p} E_p \to 0 \end{equation}

is exact, where the first map is the inclusion and the second is the evaluation map.

A proof is in contained in Jet’s book, Chapter 12.

Theorem (Extension theorem for local sections). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$ and each open neighbohrood $U$ of $p$, the natural localization map

\begin{equation}\notag \lambda_{U,p}: \cale(U) \to \cale_p \end{equation}

sending a section to the germ that it represents is surjective.

The proof uses bump functions, just like the same extension theorem for smooth functions.

Theorem (Stalks of vector bundles are free). Let $E$ be a rank-$k$ vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$, the stalk $\cale_p$ is a free $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module of rank $k$. In particular, if $U$ is an open neighborhood of $p$ over which $E$ trivializes, then the natural map

\begin{equation}\label{nat-eqn} C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty(U)} \cale(U) \to \cale_p, \quad f \otimes \sigma \mapsto f\lambda_{U,p}(\sigma), \end{equation}

is an isomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules, where $\lambda_{U,p}$ is the natural localization map from the previous theorem.

We have already observed that a choice of local frame

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \cale(U) \end{equation}

forms a basis of $\cale(U)$ as a $C^\infty(U)$-module, and that the localization map $\lambda_{U,p}$ is surjective. Thus, we need only show that the map \eqref{nat-eqn} is injective. So, suppose that we have an element $f^i \otimes \vartheta_i$ in the domain of \eqref{nat-eqn}, with summation over repeated indices implied. Suppose that it is sent to zero in the stalk, so that we have $f^i \vartheta_i = 0$. (Be very careful to understand exactly what this equation means, as I am confusing sections with germs again, and have omitted the localization map.) By shrinking $U$ to a smaller neighborhood $V$ of $p$ if needed, we may suppose that this equation actually occurs in the module $\cale(V)$, where now $f^i \in C^\infty(V)$. But the sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k$ are linearly independent in $\cale(V)$ as well, and hence $f^i = 0$ on $V$ for each $i$. Hence $f^i=0$ in $C^\infty_{M,p}$, which is what we wanted to prove. Q.E.D.
